Question: The two nearest harmonics of a tube closed at one end and open at other end are 220 Hz and 260 Hz. What is the fundamental frequency of the system?
Solution:
Two successive frequencies of closed pipe
$\frac{{nv}}{{4l}} = 220$ … (i)
$\frac{{\left( {n + 2} \right)v}}{{4l}} = 260$ … (ii)
Dividing (ii) by (i), we get
$\frac{{n + 2}}{n} = \frac{{260}}{{220}} = \frac{{13}}{{11}}$
$11n + 22 = 13n$
$n = 11$
So, $11\frac{v}{{4l}} = 220$
$\frac{v}{{4l}} = 20$
So fundamental frequency is 20 Hz.

Question: Two cities X and Y are connected by a regular bus service with a bus leaving in either direction every T min. A girl is driving scotty with a speed of 60 km/h in the direction X to Y notices that a bus goes past her every 30 minutes in the direction of her motion, and every 10 minutes in the opposite direction. Choose the correct option for the period T of the bus service and the speed (assumed constant) of the buses.
Solution:
Speed of the bus $ = V\,km/h$
While the girl is driving the scooty with speed 60 km/hr
From X to Y direction bus goes past her every 30 minutes in the direction of her motion 10 minutes in the opposite direction.
Bus leaves at every T min w.r.t of ground distance between two buses
$d = VT = V \times \frac{T}{{60}}km\,\,\,..\left( 1 \right)$
w.r.t of max (X to Y) in same direction ${V_{B/m}} = \left( {V - 60} \right)$
Distance $D = \left( {V - 60} \right)\frac{{30}}{{60}}\,\,..\left( 2 \right)$
In opposite direction ${V_{B/m}} = v + 60$
$D = \left( {V + 60} \right)\frac{{10}}{{60}}\,\,\,....\left( 3 \right)$
$\left( 1 \right) = \left( 3 \right)$
$\left( {V - 60} \right)\frac{{30}}{{60}} = \left( {V + 60} \right)\frac{{10}}{{60}}$
$3V - 180 = V + 60$
$2V = 240$
$V = 120km/hr$
$\left( 1 \right) = \left( 2 \right)$
$\frac{{VT}}{{60}} = \frac{{\left( {V - 60} \right)30}}{{60}}$
$120 \times T = \left( {20 - 60} \right)30$
$120 \times T = 60 \times 30$
$T = 15\min $

Question:
Which of the following reactions are disproportionation reaction?
(i) \[2C{u^ + } \to C{u^{2 + }} + C{u^0}\]
(ii) \[3MnO_4^{2 - } + 4{H^ + } \to 2MnO_4^ - + Mn{O_2} + 2{H_2}O\]
(iii) \[2KMn{O_4}\xrightarrow{\Delta }{K_2}Mn{O_4} + Mn{O_2} + {O_2}\]
(iv) \[2MnO_4^ - + 3M{n^{2 + }} + 2{H_2}O \to 5Mn{O_2} + 4{H^ \oplus }\]
Select the correct option from the following:
Solution:
(a) \[\mathop {2C{u^{ + 1}}}\limits^{ + 1} \to \mathop {C{u^{2\left( + \right)}}}\limits^{ + 2} + \mathop {C{u^0}}\limits^0 \} \,Disproportionation\]
(b) \[\mathop {3MnO_4^{2 - }}\limits^{ + 6} + 4{H^ + } \to \mathop {2MnO_4^ - }\limits^{ + 7} + \mathop {Mn{O_2}}\limits^{ + 4} + 2{H_2}O\} \,Disproportionation\]
(c) \[\mathop {2KMnO_4^{ - 2}}\limits^{ + 7} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits^{ + 6} + \mathop {Mn{O_2}}\limits^{ + 4} + \mathop {{O_2}}\limits^0 \}\]
(d) \[\mathop {2MnO_4^ - }\limits^{ + 7} + \mathop {3M{n^{2( + )}}}\limits^{ + 2} + 2{H_2}O \to \mathop {5Mn{O_2}}\limits^{ + 4} + 4{H^ \oplus }\} \]

Question: A spherical black body with a radius of 12 cm radiates 450 watt power at 500 K. If the radius were halved and the temperature doubled, the power radiated in watt would be
Solution:
Rate of power loss
$P \propto {R^2}{T^4}$                                        (where, $$P$$ power loss, $$R$$ is radius and $$T$$ is temperature)
$\frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = \frac{{R_1^2T_1^4}}{{R_2^2T_2^4}}$
$$\frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = 4 \times \frac{1}{{16}}$$
$\frac{{450}}{{{P_2}}} = \frac{1}{4}$
${P_2} = 1800\,{\text{watt}}$

Transition : 3 → 2 ⇒ Wavelength λ
Transition : 4 → 3 ⇒ Wavelength λ'=?
$$\frac{{\frac{1}{\lambda }}}{{\frac{1}{{\lambda '}}}} = \left| {\frac{{{R_H}{Z^2}\left( {\frac{1}{{{2^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{3^2}}}} \right)}}{{{R_H}{Z^2}\left( {\frac{1}{{{3^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{4^2}}}} \right)}}} \right| \Rightarrow \frac{{\lambda '}}{\lambda } = \frac{{20}}{7} \Rightarrow \lambda ' = \frac{{20\lambda }}{7}$$

Question: Consider a particle moving along a straight line, whose position as a function of time is given by $s(t)=\alpha t^{2}-\beta t +\gamma$, where $\alpha=1 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}, \beta=6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and $\gamma=5 \mathrm{~m}$. The average speed of the particle, in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ from $t=0$ to $t=6 \mathrm{~s}$ is:
Solution:
Position (s)
$S=\alpha t^{2}-\beta t+\gamma$
$$
\begin{equation*}
S=t^{2}-6 t+5 \tag{i}
\end{equation*}
$$
Velocity ( $v$ )
$$
\begin{equation*}
v=\frac{d s}{d t}=2 t-6 \tag{ii}
\end{equation*}
$$
When $v=0=2 t-6$
$t=3 \mathrm{~s}$
From (ii), graph between ( $v / t$ )

For distance
$d=\left(+A_{1}\right)+A_{2}$
$d=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 6+\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 6$
$d=3 \times 6=18$
Average speed $\left(v_{\text {avg }}\right)=\frac{\text { Distance }}{\text { Time }}=\frac{d}{\Delta t}=\frac{18}{6}=3 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$

Question: A wave travelling in the +ve x-direction having maximum displacement along y-direction as 1 m, wavelength $$2\pi $$ m and frequency of $$\frac{1}{\pi }Hz$$ is represented by
Solution:
The equation of a wave travelling along the x-axis is
y=asin(ωt-kx) or
y=asin(kx-ωt)
Given that  $$\lambda  = 2\pi {\text{ }}m{\text{     ;     }}{\mkern 1mu} f = \frac{1}{\pi }Hz$$
\[\therefore k = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } = \frac{{2\pi }}{{2\pi }} = 1\]
\[\omega  = 2\pi f = 2\pi  \times \frac{1}{\pi } = 2\]
 $$ a  = 1\,m\,\,(given\,\,amplitude)$$
\[\therefore y = sin(x - 2t)\]

\[\sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta }  = \sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2} - 2AB\cos \theta } \]

\[\therefore \cos \theta  = 0\]

Question: A particle of mass m is moving around the origin with a constant force F pulling it towards the origin. If Bohr model is used to describe its motion, the radius of the nth orbit and the particle’s speed $v$ in the orbit depend on n as
Solution:
Bohr’s quantization
(1) $L = mvr = nh,v = \frac{{nh}}{{mr}}$
(2) F = constant
$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = F$
$\frac{m}{r}\left( {\frac{{{n^2}{h^2}}}{{{m^2}{r^2}}}} \right) = F$
${n^2} \propto {r^3}$
${n^{\frac{2}{3}}} \propto r$
$\because v = \frac{{nh}}{{mr}}$
$v \propto \frac{n}{r} \propto \frac{n}{{{n^{2/3}}}} \propto {n^{\frac{1}{3}}}$

Question:
Match List - I with List - II.
List – IList – II
(a) [Fe(CN)6]3-(i) 5.92 B.M.
(b) [Fe(H2O)6]3+(ii) 0 B.M.
(c) [Fe(CN)6]4-(iii) 4.90 B.M.
(d) [Fe(H2O)6]2+(iv) 1.73 B.M.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
Solution:
(a) [Fe(CN)6]3-: Fe3+ = [Ar] 3d5. In presence of strong field CN–, electron pairing takes place and the number of unpaired electrons = 1. Thus, \[\mu = \sqrt 3 \;{\text{B}}{\text{.M}}{\text{.}}\]
(b) [Fe(H2O)6]3+: Fe3+ = [Ar] 3d5. In presence of weak field H2O, electron pairing does not take place and the number of unpaired electrons = 5. Thus, \[\mu = \sqrt {35} \;{\text{B}}{\text{.M}}{\text{.}}\]
(c) [Fe(CN)6]4–: Fe2+ = [Ar] 3d6
In the presence of strong field CN– ligand, electrons will be paired and \[\mu = 0\;{\text{B}}{\text{.M}}{\text{.}}\]
(d) [Fe(H2O)6]2+: Fe2+ = [Ar] 3d6. In presence of weak field H2O, electron pairing does not take place and the number of unpaired electrons = 4. Thus, \[\mu = \sqrt {24} \;{\text{B}}{\text{.M}}{\text{.}}\]

Question: Two pith balls carrying equal charges are suspended from a common point by strings of equal length; the equilibrium separation between them is r. Now the strings are rigidly clamped at half the height. The equilibrium separation between the balls now become.
Solution:
In the equilibrium position
Tcosθ = mg
\[Tsin\theta = {F_e} = \frac{{k{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\]
\[\therefore \tan \theta = \frac{{k{q^2}}}{{{r^2}mg}}\]
From the diagram, we get
\[\therefore \tan \theta = \frac{{r/2}}{y}\]
\[\therefore \frac{{r/2}}{y} = \frac{{k{q^2}}}{{{r^2}mg}}\]
\[ \to y = \frac{{{r^3}mg}}{{2k{q^2}}}\]
\[y\, \propto \,{r^3}\]
Or, \[r \propto {y^{\frac{1}{3}}}\] 
The equilibrium separation for (y/2) is given as
 \[r' \propto {\left( {\frac{y}{2}} \right)^{1/3}}\] ( as : -\[r \propto {y^{\frac{1}{3}}}\] )
\[ \to r'\, = \frac{r}{{{2^{1/3}}}}\]

Question: A black body is at a temperature of 5760 K. The energy of radiation emitted by the body at wavelength 250 nm is U<sub>1</sub> , at wavelength 500 nm is U<sub>2</sub> and that at 1000 nm is U<sub>3</sub>. Wien’s constant b = 2.88 × 10<sup>6</sup> nm-K. Which of the following is correct?
Solution:
Maximum amount of emitted radiation corresponding to \[{\lambda _m} = \frac{b}{T}\]
\[{\lambda _m} = \frac{{2.88 \times {{10}^6}nm - K}}{{5760K}} = 500nm\]

From the graph U1 < U2 > U3

Question: The number of carbon atoms per unit cell of diamond unit cell is
Solution:
The number of atoms present in a diamond cubit unit cell
\[ = \frac{1}{8} \times 8 + \frac{1}{2} \times 6 + 4 = 8\]atoms

Question: The efficiency of an ideal heat engine working between the freezing point and boiling point of water, is
Solution:
Efficiency of ideal heat engine, $\eta = \left( {1 - \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}} \right)$
T2 : Sink temperature
T1 : Source temperature
$\% \eta = \left( {1 - \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}} \right) \times 100$
$ = \left( {1 - \frac{{273}}{{373}}} \right) \times 100$
$ = \left( {\frac{{100}}{{373}}} \right) \times 100 = 26.8\% $

Question: Which of the following is an analgesic ?
Solution:
An analgesic or painkiller is any member of the group of drugs used to achieve analgesia, relief from pain. Analgesic drugs act in various ways on the peripheral and central nervous systems. They are distinct from anesthetics, which temporarily affect, and in some instances completely eliminate, sensation.

Morphine (Narcotic analgesics) and many of its homologues, when administered in medicinal doses, relieve pain and produce sleep. In poisonous doses, these produce stupor, coma, convulsions and ultimately death. Morphine narcotics are sometimes referred to as opiates, since they are obtained from the opium poppy.

Chloromycetin medicine is used to treat bacterial eye infections.

Penicillin is an antibiotic drug.

Streptomycin belongs to a class of drugs known as aminoglycoside antibiotics. It works by killing the organisms that cause the infection.

Question:
For the reaction A(g) ⇌ 2B(g), the backward reaction rate constant is higher than the forward reaction rate constant by a factor of 2500, at 1000 K.
[Given: R = 0.0831 L atm mol–1 K–1] Kp for the reaction at 1000 K is
Solution:
\[{\text{A}}\left( {\text{g}} \right) \rightleftharpoons 2{\text{B}}\left( {\text{g}} \right)\]
\[{{\text{r}}_{\text{f}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}\left[ {\text{A}} \right]\]
\[{{\text{r}}_{\text{b}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{\left[ {\text{B}} \right]^2}\]
At eq.
\[{{\text{r}}_{\text{f}}} = {{\text{r}}_{\text{b}}}\]
\[{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}\left[ {\text{A}} \right] = {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{\left[ {\text{B}} \right]^2}\]
\[{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}\left[ {\text{A}} \right] = 2500 \times {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}{\left[ {\text{B}} \right]^2}\]
\[\frac{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}}}{{2500 \times {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}}} = \frac{{{{\left[ {\text{B}} \right]}^2}}}{{\left[ {\text{A}} \right]}}\]
\[{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = \frac{1}{{2500}}{\text{mol}}/{\text{lit}}\]
\[{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}{\left[ {{\text{RT}}} \right]^{\Delta {{\text{n}}_{\text{g}}}}}\]
\[{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}} = 0.03324\,{\text{atm}}\]

Question: A conducting loop of finite resistance lies on the $x-y$ plane. There is a constant magnetic field in the $z$ direction. The area of the loop varies with time $t$, as $A=A_{0}(1+\sin t)$ in appropriate units. The figure that correctly indicates the qualitative behaviour of the power $P$ dissipated in the loop as a function of time is:
Solution:
$A=A_{0}(1+\sin t)$
$\phi=B A=B A_{0}(1+\sin t)$
$\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-B A_{0} \cos t$
$l_{\text {induced }}=\frac{\varepsilon_{\text {ind }}}{R}=-\frac{B A_{0}}{R} \cos t$
Power $=P=I^{2} R=\frac{B^{2} A_{0}^{2} \cos ^{2} t}{R}$
$P \propto \cos ^{2} t$

Question: The following four wires are made of the same material. Which among these will have the largest extension when the same tension is applied?
Solution:
Young’s modulus for a wire is
\[Y = \frac{{MgL}}{{A\Delta L}}\]=constant
\[\therefore \Delta L = \frac{{MgL}}{{AY}}\]
Or , \[\Delta L \propto \frac{L}{A}\]
Among the given options, the ratio of \[\frac{L}{A}\] is maximum for L = 50 cm and D = 0.5 mm.
Hence, option (a) is correct.

Question: Hot concentrated sulphuric acid is a moderately strong oxidizing agent. Which of the following reactions does not show oxidizing behavior?
Solution:
CaF2 + H2SO4 →CaSO4 + 2HF
In this reaction, oxidation number of none of the atom is changed. Hence H2SO4 is not acting as oxidising agent.

Question: An electric dipole is placed at an angle of 30<sup>o</sup> with an electric field intensity 2×10<sup>5</sup>N/C. It experiences a torque equal to 4 Nm. The charge on the dipole, if the dipole length is 2 cm is
Solution:

$$C = q\ell {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} E\sin \theta $$
$$4 = q \times 2 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times 2 \times {10^5}\sin 30^\circ $$
$ \Rightarrow q = 2mC$

Question: When an α-particle of mass ‘m’ moving with velocity v bombards on a heavy nucleus of charge ‘Ze’, its distance of closest approach from the nucleus depends on m as
Solution:
At closest distance of approach, the kinetic energy of the particle will convert completely into electrostatic potential energy.
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{{KQq}}{d} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,d \propto \frac{1}{m} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The equilibrium concentrations of the species in the reaction $A + B \rightleftharpoons C + D$ are 2, 3, 10 and 6 mol L<sup>–1</sup>. respectively at 300 K. ∆ G° for the reaction is (R = 2 cal/mol K)
Solution:
$A + B \rightleftharpoons C + D$
[A] = 2 mol L–1
[B] = 3 mol L–1
[C] = 10 mol L–1
[D] = 6 mol L–1
$\begin{gathered}
  \Delta {{\text{G}}^{\text{0}}} = - 2.303{\text{RT}}\log {{\text{K}}_{eq}} \\
   = - 2.303{\text{RT}}\log \frac{{[{\text{C}}][{\text{D}}]}}{{[{\text{A}}][{\text{B}}]}} \\
   = - 2.303 \times 2 \times 300 \times \log \frac{{10 \times 6}}{{2 \times 3}} \\
   = - 2.303 \times 2 \times 300 \times \log 10 \\
   = - 1381.8{\text{ cal}} \\
\end{gathered} $

$\begin{gathered}
   = 12.56V \\

Question: A 800 turn coil of effective area 0.05 m<sup>2</sup> is kept perpendicular to a magnetic field 5 × 10<sup>–5</sup> T. When the plane of the coil is rotated by 90° around any of its coplanar axis in 0.1 s, the emf induced in the coil will be:
Solution:
Magnetic field B = 5 × 10-5 T
Number of turns in coil N = 800
Area of coil A = 0.05 m2
Time taken to rotate ∆t = 0.1 s
Initial angle θ1 = 0º
Final angle θ2 = 90º
Change in magnetic flux $$\Delta \phi $$
= NBAcos90º - NBAcos0º
= - NBA
= - 800 × 5 × 10-5 × 0.05
= -2 × 10-3 weber
\[e = - \frac{{\Delta \phi }}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{ - \left( - \right)2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}Wb}}{{0.1s}} = 0.02V\].

Question: A thin flat circular disc of radius 4.5 cm is placed gently over the surface of water. If surface of water. If surface tension of water is $0.07N{m^{ - 1}}$, then the excess force required to take it away from the surface is:
Solution:
Excess Force $ = T.2\pi R$

Question: The order of stability of the following tautomeric compounds is
Solution:
The order of stability of the tautomeric compounds is III > II > I

The tautomer III has the highest stability as the C = C double bond and C = O double bond are conjugated which results in delocalization and hydrogen bonding.

Tautomer I is less stable than tautomer II as the enol is less stable than keto group.

Question: Two identical bodies are made of a material for which the heat capacity increases with temperature. One of these is at 100<sup>o</sup>C, while the other one is at 0<sup>o</sup> C. If the two bodies are brought into contact, then assuming no heat loss, the final common temperature is
Options: less than 50 C but greater than 0<sup>o</sup>C
Solution:
Let \[\theta \] be the final common temperature. Further, let Sc and Sh be the average heat capacities of the cold and hot (initially) bodies respectively (where Sc < Sh given)
From, principle of calorimetry,
Heat lost = Heat gained
$${S_h} \times \left( {100^\circ C - \theta } \right) = {S_c}\theta $$
$\therefore \theta = \frac{{{S_h}}}{{\left( {{S_h} + {S_c}} \right)}} \times 100^\circ C = \frac{{100^\circ C}}{{\left( {1 + \frac{{{S_c}}}{{{S_h}}}} \right)}}$
$\because \frac{{{S_c}}}{{{S_h}}} < 1\,\,\,\,\therefore 1 + \frac{{{S_c}}}{{{S_h}}} < 2$
$\therefore \theta > \frac{{100^\circ C}}{2}$ or $\theta > 50^\circ C$

Question: When light propagates through a material medium of relative permittivity є<sub>r</sub>, and   relative permeabilityє<sub>r</sub>,  the velocity of light, v is given by (c - velocity of light in vacuum)
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  C = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\varepsilon _0}{\mu _0}} }} \\
  v = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\varepsilon _m}{\mu _m}} }} \\
   = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\varepsilon _0}{\varepsilon _r}{\mu _0}{\mu _r}} }} \\
  v = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\varepsilon _0}{\mu _0}} }} \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\varepsilon _r}{\mu _r}} }} = \boxed{\frac{C}{{\sqrt {{\varepsilon _r}{\mu _r}} }}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Aspirin and Paracetamol belong to the class of narcotic analgesics.
Statement II: Morphine and Heroin are non-narcotic analgesics. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
Options: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is false
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Solution:
Aspirin and Paracetamol belong to the class of non-narcotic analgesics.
Morphine and Heroin are narcotic analgesics.

Question: An air column, closed at one end and open at the other, resonates with a tuning fork when the smallest length of the column is 50 cm. The next larger length of the column resonating with the same tuning fork is
Solution:
First minimum resonating length for closed organ pipe \[ = \frac{\lambda }{4} = 50cm\]
$\therefore $ Next larger length of air column \[ = \frac{{3\lambda }}{4} = 150cm\]

Question: A coil of self-inductance L is connected in series with a bulb B and an AC source. Brightness of the bulb decreases when
Options: Frequency of the AC source is decreased
Number of turns in the coil is reduced
A capacitance of reactance X<sub>C</sub> = X<sub>L</sub> is included in the same circuit
An iron rod is inserted in the coil
Solution:
Impedance is
\[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {X^2}_L} = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{(L \times 2\pi f)}^2}} \]
\[I = \frac{E}{Z}\]
\[I = \frac{E}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {L \times 2\pi f} \right)}^2}} }}\]

Brightness of bulb decreases if \[I \downarrow \]

So \[I \downarrow \] if  \[ \,L \uparrow ,\,f \uparrow \]

and we know 

\[L \propto M\]

If an iron rod is inserted in the coil then

\[M \uparrow ,\,L \uparrow ,\,I \downarrow \]

Question: Major products A and B formed in the following reaction sequence, are

A capillary tube of radius r is immersed in water and water rises in it to a height h. The mass of the water in the capillary is $5\,gm$. Another capillary tube of radius 2r is immersed in water. The mass of water that will rise in this tube is :
Solution:
\[h = \frac{{2T{\mkern 1mu} \cos \theta }}{{\rho rg}}\]
\[m = \rho  \times volume{\mkern 1mu}  = {\mkern 1mu} \rho  \times h \times \pi {r^{\bf{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu}  = \;{\mkern 1mu} 5\,gm\]
\[r' = 2r\]
and \[ \;\;{\mkern 1mu} h' = h/2;\]
\[m' = \rho  \times \frac{h}{2} \times \pi {\mkern 1mu} {(2r)^{\bf{2}}}\]
   
m'\[{\mathbf{ = }}\rho {\mathbf{ \times }}h\pi {r^{\mathbf{2}}}{\mathbf{ \times }}2{\mathbf{ = }}10\,gm\]

Question: An iron rod of susceptibility 599 is subjected to a magnetising field of 1200 A\[{m^{ - {\mathbf{1}}}}.\] The permeability of the material of the rod is\[:({\mu _{\mathbf{0}}}{\mathbf{ = }}4\pi {\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{ - {\mathbf{7}}}}\,T\,\,m{A^{ - {\mathbf{1}}}})\]
Solution:
\[{\chi _{\mathbf{m}}}{\mathbf{ = }}599,\;{\mu _{\mathbf{0}}}{\mathbf{ = }}4\pi {\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{ - {\mathbf{7}}}}\]
\[H{\mathbf{ = }}1200\,A/m,\;\;\mu {\mathbf{ = }}?\]
\[\mu {\mathbf{ = }}{\mu _{\mathbf{0}}}(1{\mathbf{ + }}{\chi_{\mathbf{m}}})\]
\[{\mathbf{ = }}4\pi {\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{ - {\mathbf{7}}}}(1{\mathbf{ + }}599)\]
\[{\mathbf{ = }}4\pi {\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{ - {\mathbf{7}}}}{\mathbf{ \times }}600\]
\[{\mathbf{ = }}24\pi {\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{ - {\mathbf{5}}}}\]
\[\mu {\mathbf{ = }}\,2.4\pi {\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{ - {\mathbf{4}}}}\,\,T\,m\,{A^{ - {\mathbf{1}}}}\]

Question: Two identical charged spheres suspended from a common point by two massless strings of lengths 1, are initially at a distance d (d <<1) apart because of their mutual repulsion. The charges begin to leak from both the spheres at a constant rate. As a result, the spheres approach each other with a velocity v. Then v varies as a function of the distance x between the spheres, as
Solution:
\[\tan \theta = \frac{{{F_e}}}{{mg}}  \]
\[\frac{{K{q^2}}}{{{x^2}mg}} = \frac{x}{{2\ell }}\]
Or x3 ∝ q2…(1)
Or \[{x^{\frac{3}{2}}} \propto q\]…(2)
differentiate eq.(i) w.r.t. time 
\[3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} \propto 2q\frac{{dq}}{{dt}}\]    but 
\[\frac{{dq}}{{dt}}\] is constant
so \[{x^2}\; \times v{\rm{ }} \propto {\rm{ }}q\]
 replace q from eq.(2)
\[{x^2}\left( v \right) \propto {x^{\frac{3}{2}}}\] or \[v \propto {x^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}\]

Question: If the velocity of a particle is v = At + Bt<sup>2</sup>, where A and B are constants, then the distance travelled by it between 1s and 2s is
Solution:
\[v = At + B{t^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = At + B{t^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \int\limits_0^x {dx}  = \int\limits_1^2 {\left( {At + B{t^2}} \right)} {\mkern 1mu} dt\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} x = \frac{A}{2}\left( {{2^2} - {1^2}} \right) + \frac{B}{3}\left( {{2^3} - {1^3}} \right)\]

\[ = \frac{{3A}}{2} + \frac{{7B}}{3}\]

Question: The wettability of the surface by a liquid depends primarily on

Question: A bar magnet of length l and magnetic dipole moment M is bent in the form of an arc as shown in figure. The new magnetic dipole moment will be
Solution:
Let magnetic pole strength be m, then
M = ml
In the second case,
\[l = r\theta = r\left( {\frac{\pi }{3}} \right)\]
\[r = \frac{{3l}}{\pi }\]
Now,
\[m' = mr = m\left( {\frac{{3l}}{\pi }} \right)\]
\[ \to M' = \frac{{3ml}}{\pi }\]
\[\therefore M' = \frac{{3M}}{\pi }\]

Question: The maximum elongation of a steel wire of 1m length if the elastic limit of steel and its Young’s modulus, respectively, are $8 \times {10^8}N{m^{ - 2}}$ and $2 \times {10^{11}}N{m^{ - 2}}$, is
Solution:
Stress = Y . strain
Stress$ = \frac{{Y{{\left( {\Delta L} \right)}_{\max }}}}{L}$
$8 \times {10^8} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{11}} \times {{\left( {\Delta L} \right)}_{\max }}}}{1}$
${\left( {\Delta L} \right)_{\max }} = 4 \times {10^{ - 3}}meter = 4mm$

Question: In the measurement of viscosity of liquids using terminal velocity experiment, spherical balls of same radius but having different densities are used. The variation of the terminal velocity ( $v$ ) with the ratio of density of spherical ball ( $\sigma$ ) to density of the liquid ( $\rho$ ), is best represented by:
Solution:
Terminal velocity $V_{T}=\frac{2 r^{2}}{9 h}(\sigma-\rho)$
$$
\begin{align*}
& V_{T}=\frac{2 r^{2}}{9 h} \rho\left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho}-1\right) \tag{I}\\
& y=m x+c \tag{II}
\end{align*}
$$

Equation number (I) is like equation number which is straight line with negative intercept.

Question: In Duma’s method for estimation of nitrogen, 0.25 g of an organic compound gave 40 mL of nitrogen collected at 300 K temperature and 725 mm pressure. If the aqueous tension at 300K is 25mm, the percentage of nitrogen in the compound is:
Solution:
0.25g 40 mL N2 at 300K, 725 mm pressure
Aqueous tension at 300 K is 25 mm
725 - 25 = 700 mm
Temperature 300 K, Mass of the sub 0.25g, Volume of moist nitrogen = 40 mL
\[\frac{{{p_1}{v_1}}}{{{T_1}}} = \frac{{{P_2}{V_2}}}{{{T_2}}}\]
\[\therefore {V_2} = \frac{{{P_1}{V_1}}}{{{T_1}}} \times \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{P_2}}} = \frac{{700 \times 40 \times 273}}{{300 \times 760}}\]
\[ = \frac{{7644000}}{{22800}} = 33.52mL\]
of nitrogen
22400 mL of nitrogen at S.T.P weighs = 28g
33.52 mL of nitrogen at S.T.P. weighs
\[ = \frac{{28 \times 33.52}}{{22400}} = \frac{{938.56}}{{22400}} = 0.0419g\]
Percentage of nitrogen in organic compound
\[ = \frac{{0.0419}}{{0.25}} \times 100\]
= 16.76%

Question: A monoatomic gas at a pressure P, having a volume V expands isothermally to a volume 2V and then adiabatically to a volume 16V. The final pressure of the gas is: \[\left( {\gamma  = \frac{5}{3}} \right)\]
Solution:
In isothermal process

$${P_1}{V_1} = {P_2}{V_2}$$

$$PV = {P_2}2V$$

$${P_2} = \frac{P}{2}$$

Now in adiabatic process

$$P{V^\gamma } = const$$

$$\frac{p}{2}{\left( {2V} \right)^\gamma } = {P_f}{\left( {16V} \right)^\gamma }$$

$${P_f} = \frac{P}{2}{\left( {\frac{{2V}}{{16V}}} \right)^\gamma }$$

$$\gamma  = \frac{5}{3}$$

\[\begin{gathered}
  \
  \frac{{{P_f}}}{P} = \frac{1}{{{2^{3\gamma + 1}}}} = \frac{1}{{{2^6}}} = \frac{1}{{64}} \\ 
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Two non-mixing liquids of densities ρ and  nρ (n > 1) are put in a container. The height of each liquid is h. A solid cylinder of length L and density d is put in this container. The cylinder floats with its axis vertical and length pL (p < 1) in the denser liquid. The density d is equal to
Solution:
\[L{\rm{ }}A{\rm{ }}d{\rm{ }}g{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}\left( {pL} \right)A\left( {n\rho } \right)g{\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}\left( {1{\rm{ }} - {\rm{ }}p} \right)LA{\rm{ }}\rho g\]
\[ \Rightarrow {\rm{ }}d{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}\left( {1{\rm{ }} - {\rm{ }}p} \right)\rho {\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}pn{\rm{ }}\rho {\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}\left[ {1{\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}\left( {n{\rm{ }} - {\rm{ }}1} \right)p} \right]\rho \]

The horizontal component remains the same, while the vertical component changes.
So, the velocity at point B is

Question: The compound that will undergo SN1 reaction with the fastest rate is

A bullet is fired from a gun at the speed of $280\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ in the direction $30^\circ $ above the horizontal. The maximum height attained by the bullet is $\left( {g = 9.8\,m{s^{ - 2}},\,\,\sin 30^\circ  = 0.5} \right):$
Ratio of longest wavelengths corresponding to Lyman and Balmer series in hydrogen spectrum is
Solution:
Rydberg formula is given as
\[\frac{1}{\lambda } = R\left( {\frac{l}{{{{(n')}^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{n^2}}}} \right)\]
For Lyman series
\[\frac{1}{\lambda } = R\left( {\frac{l}{{{1^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{2^2}}}} \right)\]
\[\frac{1}{\lambda } = \frac{{3R}}{4}\]
\[\therefore \lambda = \frac{4}{{3R}}\]…………[equation 1]
For Balmer series,
\[\therefore \frac{1}{{{\lambda _b}}} = R\left( {\frac{l}{{{2^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{3^2}}}} \right)\]
\[\frac{1}{\lambda_b } = \frac{{5R}}{{36}}\]
\[\therefore \lambda_b  = \frac{{36}}{{5R}}\]……………[equation 2]
\[\frac{\lambda }{{{\lambda _b}}} = \frac{4}{{3R}} \times \frac{{5R}}{{36}}\]
\[\therefore \frac{\lambda }{{{\lambda _b}}} = \frac{5}{{27}}\]

Question: Two identical charged conducting spheres A and B have their centres separated by a certain distance. Charge on each sphere is q and the force of repulsion between them is F. A third identical uncharged conducting sphere is brought in contact with sphere A first and then with B and finally removed from both. New force of repulsion between spheres A and B (Radii of A and B are negligible compared to the distance of separation so that for calculating force between them they can be considered as point charges) is best given as :
Solution:
Force of repulsion $F = \frac{{k{q^2}}}{{{d^2}}}$ a third identical uncharged conducting sphere is brought in constant with A sphere and then with B.

${q_1} = \frac{{q + 0}}{2} = \frac{q}{2}$
Now charge on both A and C $ = \frac{q}{2}$

${q_2} = \frac{{q + \frac{q}{2}}}{2} = \frac{{8q}}{4}$
Now force between A and B

$F' = \frac{{k{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{d^2}}} = \frac{{k\left( {\frac{q}{2}} \right)\left( {\frac{{8q}}{4}} \right)}}{{{d^2}}}$
$F' = \frac{3}{8}\frac{{k{q^2}}}{{{d^2}}} = \frac{3}{8}F$

$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = aR \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} \Rightarrow {v^2} = 32.47 \Rightarrow v \simeq 5.7m/s$

Question: The molar conductance of NaCl. HCl and CH<sub>3</sub>COONa at infinite dilution are 126.45, 426.16 and 91.0 S cm<sup>2</sup> mol<sup>-1</sup> respectively. The molar conductance of CH<sub>3</sub>COOH at infinite dilution is. Choose the right option for your answer.

201.28 S cm2 mol-1
390.71 S cm2 mol-1
Solution:
λ∞(CH3COOH) = λ∞(CH3COONa) + λ∞(HCl) - λ∞(NaCl)
= (91 + 426.16 - 126.45) S cm2 mol-1
= 390.71 S cm2 mol-1

Question:
In the first excited state of hydrogen atom, the energy of its electron is -3.4 eV . The radial distance of the electron from the hydrogen nucleus in this case is approximately:
(Take $1 \mathrm{eV}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}, \mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ and $\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$ )
Solution:
$\frac{K Q^{2}}{2 r}=3.4 \mathrm{eV}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=r \\
& 2.1176 \times 10^{-10}=r \\
& r=2.1 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: Two-point charges A and B, having charges + Q and – Q respectively are placed at certain distance apart and force acting between them is F. If 25% charge of A is transferred to B, then force between the charges becomes:
Solution:
\[F = \frac{{k{Q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\]
If 25% of charge of A transferred to B then
\[{q_A} = Q - \frac{Q}{4} = \frac{{3Q}}{4}and\,\,{q_B} = - Q + \frac{Q}{4} = \frac{{ - 3Q}}{4}\]

\[\begin{gathered}
  {F_1} = \frac{{k{q_A}{q_B}}}{{{r^2}}} \\
  {F_1} = \frac{{k{{\left( {\frac{{3Q}}{4}} \right)}^2}}}{{{r^2}}} \\
  {F_1} = \frac{9}{{16}}\frac{{kQ}}{{{r^2}}} \\
  {F_1} = \frac{{9F}}{{16}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A cup of coffee cools from 90<sup>0</sup>C to 80<sup>0</sup>C in t minutes, when the room temperature is 20<sup>0</sup>C. The time taken by a similar cup of coffee to cool from 80<sup>0</sup>C to 60<sup>0</sup>C at a room temperature same at 20<sup>0</sup>C is
Solution:
Newton’s law of cooling:
$$\eqalign{
  & - \frac{{\Delta T}}{{\Delta t}} = k\left[ {{T_{avg}} - {T_0}} \right] \cr
  & {\text{1st}}\,\,{\text{case}}:\,\,\frac{{10^\circ C}}{t} = k\left[ {85 - 20} \right] \cr
  & {\text{2nd}}\,\,{\text{case}}:\,\,\frac{{20^\circ C}}{{t'}}k\left[ {\frac{{80 + 60}}{2} - 20} \right] \cr
  & \frac{{10}}{t}.\frac{{t'}}{{20}} = \frac{{65}}{{50}} \cr
  & t' = \frac{{13t}}{5} \cr} $$

Question: If we study the vibration of a pipe open at both ends, then the following statement is not true
Options: Open end will be node of pressure wave
Odd harmonics of the fundamental frequency will be generated
All harmonic of the fundamental frequency will be generated
Pressure change will be maximum at both ends
Solution:
In case of the vibration of a pipe open at both ends, the air column can vibrate in several different nodes subjected to the boundary condition that there must be a node of pressure wave at its open end. Hence, option (1) is correct.
Ratio of frequency is v : 2v : 3v : 4v : 5v
Where \[v = \frac{V}{{2L}}\]
Both odd and even harmonic will be present. Hence option (1), (2) and (3) are correct.
The pressure variation is minimum at node of pressure wave. Hence option (4) is incorrect.

Question: An electromagnetic wave travelling in a lossless dielectric medium having a dielectric constant, $\varepsilon_{r}=9$, has the electric field, $E_{x}=E_{0} \sin \left(k z-2 \pi \times 10^{6} t\right) \vee m^{-1}$ where $E_{0}$ is the amplitude and $k$ is the wave vector. Among the following options, the incorrect choice is
Options: The wavelength of the electromagnetic wave inside the medium is 300 m
The magnetic field is given by the relation $B_{y}=\frac{B_{0}}{v} \sin \left(k z-2 \pi \times 10^{6} t\right)$ where $v$ is the speed of the electromagnetic wave inside the medium
The direction of propagation of the electromagnetic wave is along $+z$
The speed of the electromagnetic wave inside the medium is $10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
Solution:
$\Rightarrow \quad E_{x}=E_{0} \sin \left(k z-2 \pi \times 10^{6} t\right)=E_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t)$
⇒ Direction of propagation of wave is along $+z$ axis.
⇒ Speed of wave in medium
$$
\begin{aligned}
& v_{m}=\frac{v_{0}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r}}}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{9}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{3} \\
& v_{m}=10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$
⇒ Wavelength in medium $=\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}$
$$
\lambda_{m}=\frac{v_{m}}{f_{0}}=\frac{10^{8}}{\frac{2 \pi \times 10^{6}}{2 \pi}}=100 \mathrm{~m}\left[\because f_{0}=\text { constant }\right]
$$
∴ Wavelength of the electromagnetic wave inside the medium is 100 m .
Note: Among the options, in the expression $B_{y}=\frac{B_{0}}{v} \sin \left(k z-2 \pi \times 10^{6} t\right), B_{0}$ should be replaced by $E_{0}$. Otherwise option b is also incorrect

Question: Choose the correct option for graphical representation of Boyle’s law, which shows a graph of pressure vs. Volume of a gas at different temperature.

& =1.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{volt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: The approximate depth of an ocean is 2700 m. The compressibility of water is 45.4 × 10<sup>-11</sup> Pa<sup>-1</sup> and density of water is 10<sup>3</sup> kg/m<sup>3</sup>. "Find fractional change in volume”
Solution:
\[{\text{Compressibility}} = \frac{1}{{{\text{bulk modulus}}}} = \frac{{\Delta v}}{v}\frac{1}{P}\]
\[\therefore \frac{{\Delta v}}{v} = P \times {\text{compressibility}}\]
\[ = h\rho g \times {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{compressibility}}\]
=2700 × 103 × 10 × 45.4 × 10-11
=1.2 × 10 - 2

Question: Two rods A and B of different materials are welded together as shown in figure. Their thermal conductivities are K1 and K2. The thermal conductivity of the composite rod will be
Solution:
Thermal current
$H = {H_1} + {H_2}$
$$\frac{{{K_{EQ}}2A\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)}}{d} = \frac{{{K_1}A\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)}}{d} + \frac{{{K_2}A\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)}}{d}$$
$\frac{{{K_{EQ}}2A\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)}}{d} = \frac{{A\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)}}{d}\left[ {{K_1} + {K_2}} \right]$
$${K_{EQ}} = \left[ {\frac{{{K_1} + {K_2}}}{2}} \right]$$

Question: In acidic medium, H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> changes \[C{r_2}O_7^{ - 2}\] to CrO<sub>5</sub> which has two ( − O − O − ) bonds. Change in the oxidation state of chromium is
The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of 1 g of Helium in a container at NTP, from T<sub>1 </sub>K to T<sub>2 </sub>K is
Solution:
The amount of energy required to raise the temperature of n-mole of helium at NTP, from T1 K to T2 K is
$$\Delta E = \frac{f}{2}nR\left( {{T_2} - {T_1}} \right)$$
But \[{k_B} = \frac{R}{{{N_A}}}\]
\[\therefore \Delta E = \frac{f}{2}n.{N_A}{k_B}({T_2} - {T_1})\]
Where KB= Boltzman constant
\[\Delta E = \frac{3}{2}n.{N_A}{k_B}({T_2} - {T_1})\]
For He, 4 gm \[ \to 1\,mole\]
\[1gm\, \to \frac{1}{{4\,mole}}\]
\[\therefore \Delta E = \frac{3}{8}{N_A}{k_B}({T_2} - {T_1})\]

Question: A uniform magnetic field is restricted within a region of radius r. The magnetic field changes with time at a rate $\frac{{\overrightarrow {dB} }}{{dt}}$. Loop 1 of radius $R > r$ encloses the region r and loop 2 of radius R is outside the region of magnetic field as shown in the figure below. Then the e.m.f. generated is

${\varepsilon _{ind}} = - A\left( {\frac{{d\vec B}}{{dt}}} \right)\cos 0^\circ = - \pi {r^2}\left( {\frac{{d\vec B}}{{dt}}} \right)$
For Loop 2, ${\varepsilon _{ind}} = 0$ as no flux linkage

Question:
Consider that an electron is revolving in an excited state of Hydrogen atom with velocity $\sqrt{25.6} \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The radius of the orbit is $x \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $x$ is:
[Take the mass of electron to the $9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$, charge of electron $=-1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ and $\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-2}$ ]
Solution:
$F_{c}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{k q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}=\frac{m v^{2}}{r} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{k e^{2}}{r}=m v^{2} \Rightarrow r=\frac{k e^{2}}{m v^{2}}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}}{9 \times 10^{-31} \times 25.6 \times 10^{10}} \\
& \Rightarrow r=\frac{2.56 \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-38}}{25.6 \times 10^{-21}} \\
& r=1 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m} \\
& \therefore x=1
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: A pipe open at both ends has a fundamental frequency f in air. The pipe is now dipped vertically in a water drum to half of its length. The fundamental frequency of the air column is now equal to:

Before dipping $f = \frac{{nv}}{{2L}} = \frac{v}{{2L}}\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
After dipping in water \[{f_1} = \frac{{nv}}{{4\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)}} = \frac{v}{{2L}}\,\,\,....\left( 2 \right)\]
From (1) & (2)
$\boxed{{f_1} = f}$

Question: Two parallel infinite line charges with linear charge densities + λ  C/m and - λ  C/m are placed at a distance of  2R in free space. What is the electric field (magnitude only) mid-way between the two line charges?
Solution:
Electric field due to line charge (1)
\[{\vec E_1} = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}R}}\widehat iN/C\]
Electric field due to line charge (2)
\[\begin{gathered}
  {{\vec E}_2} = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}R}}\widehat iN/C \\
  {{\vec E}_{net}} = {{\vec E}_1} + {{\vec E}_2} \\
   = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}R}}\widehat i + \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}R}}\widehat i \\
   = \frac{\lambda }{{\pi {\varepsilon _0}R}}\widehat iN/C \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
On balancing the given redox reaction,
${\text{aC}}{{\text{r}}_2}{\text{O}}_7^{2 - } + {\text{bSO}}_3^{2 - }({\text{aq}}) + {\text{c}}{{\text{H}}^ + }({\text{aq}}) \to 2{\text{aC}}{{\text{r}}^{3 + }}({\text{aq}}) + {\text{bSO}}_4^{2 - }({\text{aq}}) + \frac{{\text{c}}}{2}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}(\ell )$
the coefficients a, b and c are found to be, respectively
Solution:
Reaction has to be balanced in acidic medium ‘O’ atoms are balanced by adding H2O and then H-atom is balanced by adding H+ ions and charge is balanced by ${e^ \ominus }$.
Oxidation: \[{\text{SO}}_3^{2 - } + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to {\text{SO}}_4^{2 - } + 2{{\text{H}}^ + } + 2{e^ \ominus }] \times 3\]
Reduction:\[{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_2}{\text{O}}_7^{2 - } + 14{{\text{H}}^ + } + 6{{\text{e}}^ \ominus } \to 2{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}^{3 + }} + 7{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\]
\[{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_2}{\text{O}}_7^{2 - } + 3{\text{SO}}_3^{2 - } + 8{{\text{H}}^ \oplus } \to 2{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}^{3 + }} + 3{\text{SO}}_4^{2 - } + 4{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\]
a = 1 ; b = 3 ; c = 8

Question:
The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by ${E_z} = 60\cos \left( {5x + 1.5 \times {{10}^9}t} \right)V/m$
Then expression for the corresponding magnetic field is (here subscripts denote the direction of the field):
Solution:
${E_Z} = 60\cos \left( {5x + 1.5 \times {{10}^9}t} \right)v/m$
Here, ${E_0} = 60$
${B_0} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{c} = \frac{{60}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} = 20 \times {10^{ - 8}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}$
$\vec B$ Should be perpendicular to $\vec E$
$\therefore {B_y} = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}\cos \left( {5x + 1.5 \times {{10}^9}t} \right)T$

Question: Which amongst the following options is correct graphical representation of Boyle's Law?

Boyle's law is defined at constant temperature for an ideal gas. ${\text{P}} \propto \frac{1}{{{\text{V}}}}$

slope of $P$ versus $\frac{1}{{{\text{V}}}}$ curve is ${\text{nRT}}$
$ \Rightarrow $ Slope $ \uparrow \Rightarrow {\text{T}} \uparrow \therefore {{\text{T}}_3} > {{\text{T}}_2} > {{\text{T}}_1}$

Question:
A mixture of \[{N_{\mathbf{2}}}\]and Ar gases in a cylinder 7 g of \[{N_{\mathbf{2}}}\] and 8 g of Ar. If the total pressure of the mixture of the gases in the cylinder is 27 bar, the partial pressure of \[{N_{\mathbf{2}}}\] is:
[use atomic masses (in g\[mo{l^{ - {\mathbf{1}}}}\]): N = 14, Ar = 40]

According to Dalton’s law
\[{P_{{{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathbf{2}}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}{\chi _{{{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathbf{2}}}}}{P_{{\mathbf{total}}}}\]
\[{P_{{{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathbf{2}}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{\left( {\frac{7}{{28}}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{8}{{40}}{\mathbf{ + }}\frac{7}{{28}}} \right)}}{\mathbf{ \times }}27\]
\[{P_{{{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathbf{2}}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}\left( {\frac{{0.25}}{{0.2{\mathbf{ + }}0.25}}} \right){\mathbf{ \times }}27\]
\[{P_{{{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathbf{2}}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}15\,\]bar

Question: A toy car with charge q moves on a frictionless horizontal plane surface under the influence of a uniform electric field $\vec E$. Due to the force q $\vec E$, its velocity increases from 0 to 6 m/s in one second duration. At that instant the direction of the field is reversed. The car continues to move for two more seconds under the influence of this field. The average velocity and the average speed of the toy car between 0 to 3 seconds are respectively
Solution:
Acceleration $a = \frac{{6 - 0}}{1} = 6\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
For t = 0 to t = 1s,
${S_1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 6{\left( 1 \right)^2} = 3m$ … (i)
For t = 1s to t = 2s,
${S_2} = 6.1 - \frac{1}{2} \times 6{\left( 1 \right)^2} = 3m$ … (ii)
For t = 2s to t = 3s,
${S_3} = 0 - \frac{1}{2} \times 6{\left( 1 \right)^2} = - 3m$ … (iii)
Total displacement S = S1 + S2 + S3 = 3 m
Average velocity$ = \frac{3}{3} = 1m{s^{ - 1}}$
Total distance travelled = 9 m
Average speed $ = \frac{9}{3} = 3\,m{s^{ - 1}}$

Question: A gas at 350 K and 15 bar has molar volume 20 percent smaller than that for an ideal gas under the same conditions. The correct option about the gas and its compressibility factor (Z) is :
Options: Z > 1 and repulsive forces are dominant
Z < 1 and attractive forces are dominant
Z < 1 and repulsive forces are dominant
Z > 1 and attractive forces are dominant
Solution:
*Compressibility factor (Z) \[ = \frac{{{V_{real}}}}{{{V_{ideal}}}}\]
\[\because \,\,{V_{real}} < {V_{ideal}};Hence\,Z < 1\]
* If Z < 1, attractive forces are dominant among gaseous molecules and liquefaction of gas will be easy.

Question: A car starts from rest and accelerates at 5 m/s<sup>2</sup>. At t = 4s, a ball is dropped out of a window by a person sitting in the car. What is the velocity and acceleration of the ball at t = 6 s? (Take g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>)
Solution:
$$\eqalign{
  & At\,\,t = 4,v = 0 + 5 \times 4 = 20\,\,m/s \cr
  & {v_y} = 0 + 10 \times 2 = 20 \cr
  & v = \sqrt {{{20}^2} + {{20}^2}} = 20\sqrt 2 ,\,\,a = 10\,\,m/{s^2} \cr} $$

\[\begin{gathered}

Question: A particle is moving such that its position coordinates (x, y) are (2m, 3m) at time t = 0, (6m, 7m) at time t = 2s and (13m, 14m) at time t = 5s. Average velocity vector \[\left( {{{\vec V}_{av}}} \right)\] from t = 0 to t = 5s is.

Question: A projectile is fired from the surface of the earth with a velocity of 5 ms<sup>-1</sup> and angle θ with the horizontal. Another projectile fired from another planet with a velocity of 3 ms<sup>-1</sup> at the same angle follows a trajectory which is identical with the trajectory of the projectile fired from the earth. The value of the acceleration due to gravity on the planet is (in ms<sup>-2</sup> ) : (given g = 9.8 ms<sup>-2</sup> )
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  y = x\tan \theta - \frac{1}{2}g\frac{x}{{{u^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta }}\left[ {{\text{Trajectory equation}}} \right] \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{{g_1}}}{{{{\left( {{u_1}} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{{g_2}}}{{{{\left( {{u_2}} \right)}^2}}} \\
  \therefore \,\,\frac{{{g_2}}}{{{g_1}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{u_2}}}{{{u_1}}}} \right)^2} = {\left( {\frac{3}{5}} \right)^2} = \frac{9}{{25}} \\
  \therefore \,\,\,\,{g_2} = \frac{9}{{25}} \times 9.8 = 3.5\,\,\,m/{s^2} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
A wind with speed 40 m/s blows parallel to the roof of a house. The area of the roof is 250 m2. Assuming that the pressure inside the house is atmospheric pressure, the force exerted by the wind on the roof and the direction of the force will be:
($${\rho _{air}}\; = {\text{ }}1.2\;kg/m{\;^3}$$)
Solution:
From Bernoulli’s equation
$${P_0} = P + \frac{1}{2}\rho {v^2}$$
Force will act due to pressure difference
$$\therefore {P_0} - P = \frac{1}{2}\rho {v^2}$$
$$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times {(40)^2}$$
= 0.0096 × 105
∴ Force acting upwards
F = 0.0096 × 105 × 250
= 2.4 × 105 N upwards

Question: A Carnot engine has an efficiency of 50% when its source is at a temperature 327°C. The temperature of the sink is:
Solution:
Efficiency of carnot engine
$\% \eta = \left( {1 - \frac{{{T_{{\text{sink}}}}}}{{{T_{{\text{source}}}}}}} \right) \times 100$
${T_{{\text{source}}}} = 327^\circ C = 600\,K$
$50 = \left( {1 - \frac{{{T_{{\text{sink}}}}}}{{600}}} \right) \times 100$
$\frac{1}{2} = 1 - \frac{{{T_{{\text{sink}}}}}}{{600}}$
${T_{{\text{sink}}}} = 300\,K$
So temp. of sink is $^\circ C = 300 - 2763 = 27^\circ C$

Question: Two cars P and Q start from a point at the same time in a straight line and their positions are represented by ${X_P}\left( t \right) = at + b{t^2}$ and ${X_Q}\left( t \right) = ft - {t^2}$. At what time do the cars have the same speed?

${X_P}\left( t \right) = at + b{t^2};\,{X_q}\left( t \right) = ft - {t^2}$
${V_P} = a + 2bt;\,{V_Q} = f - 2t$
as ${V_P} = {V_Q}$

$ \Rightarrow t = \frac{{f - a}}{{2\left( {1 + b} \right)}}$

Question: The x and y coordinates of the particle at any time are x = 5t – 2t2 and y = 10t respectively, where x and y are in meter and t in second. The acceleration of the particle at t = 2 s is
Solution:
$$x = 5t - 2{t^2}{\mkern 1mu} ;{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} y = 10t$$
$$\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 5 - 4t{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ;\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 10$$
$${v_x} = 5 - 4t{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ;{\mkern 1mu} {v_y} = 10$$
$$\frac{{d{v_x}}}{{dt}} =  - 4{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ;\frac{{d{v_y}}}{{dt}} = 0$$
$${a_x} =  - 4{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ;{a_y} = 0$$
Acceleration of particle at $t = 2\,s$ is $ = - 4\,m/{s^2}$

Question: Two thin dielectric slabs of dielectric constants K1 and K2 (K1 < K2) are inserted between plates of a parallel plate capacitor, as shown in the figure. The variation of electric field ‘E’ between the plates with distance ‘d’ as measured from plate P is correctly shown by:
Solution:
A uniform electric field is present between the plates of capacitors in case of absence of dielectric material. 

When a dielectric material is inserted in between the plates of capacitor, the dielectric becomes electrically polarized. The polarization charges induced on the two faces of the slab produce their own electric field $E'_0$, which opposes the external field $E_0$. Hence, the resultant field $E$ within the dielectric is smaller than $E_0$ but is in the same direction as $E_0$ and is given by $E=\frac{E_0}{K}$. So in the given question the electric field in the region of dielectric will be less as compared to other regions but will not be zero. 

As $K_2 >K_1$, the drop in the electric field for $K_2$ dielectric is more than $K_1$.

Question: In a plane electromagnetic wave travelling in free space, the electric field component oscillates sinusoidally at a frequency of $2.0 \times {10^{10}}$ Hz and amplitude 48 Vm<sup>-1</sup>. Then the amplitude of oscillating magnetic field is : (Speed of light in free space $3 \times {10^8}m{s^{ - 1}}$)
Solution:
$C = \frac{{{E_0}}}{{{B_0}}}$
${B_0} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{C}$
$\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{{48}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} \\
   = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 7}}T \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A uniform rope of length L and mass m<sub>1</sub> hangs vertically from a rigid support. A block of mass m<sub>2</sub> is attached to the free end of the rope. A transverse pulse of wavelength λ<sub>1</sub> is produced at the lower end of the rope. The wavelength of the pulse when it reaches the top of the rope is λ<sub>2</sub>. The ratio \[\frac{{{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1}}}\] is
Solution:
T1 = m2g
T2 = (m1 + m2)g
Velocity \[ \propto \sqrt T \]
\[\lambda \propto \sqrt T \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{{\lambda _1}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} = \frac{{\sqrt {{T_1}} }}{{\sqrt {{T_2}} }} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}{{{m_2}}}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Two conducting circular loops of radii R1 and R2 are placed in the same plane with their centres coinciding. If R1 >> R2, the mutual inductance M between them will be directly proportional to:
Solution:
$$\eqalign{
  & {B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}}}{{2{R_1}}} \cr
  & {\phi _2} = {B_1}\left( {\pi R_2^2} \right) = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi R_2^2}}{{2{R_1}}}{I_1} \cr
  & {\phi _2} = M{I_1}\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,M = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi R_2^2}}{{2{R_1}}} \cr
  & M \propto \frac{{R_2^2}}{{{R_1}}} \cr} $$

Question: Which one of the following statements is not correct?
Options: Coenzymes increase the catalytic activity of enzyme
Catalyst does not initiate any reaction.
The value of equilibrium constant is changed in the presence of a catalyst
Solution:
A catalyst decreases activation energies of both the forward and backward reaction by same amount, therefore, it speeds up both forward and backward reaction by same rate.
Equilibrium constant is therefore not affected by catalyst at a given temperature.

Question:
A 20 litre container at 400 K contains CO2(g) at pressure 0.4 atm and an excess of SrO (neglect the volume of solid SrO). The volume of the container is now decreased by moving the movable piston fitted in the container. The maximum volume of the container, when pressure of CO2 attains its maximum value will be.
(Given that : SrCO3(s) = SrO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 1.6 atm)
Solution:
Max. pressure of CO2 = Pressure of CO2 at equilibrium
For reaction,
SrCO3(s) ⇌ SrO(s) + CO2
Kp = PCO2 = 1.6 atm = maximum pressure of CO2
Volume of container at this stage,
$V = \frac{{nRT}}{P}$ … (i)
Since container is sealed and reaction was not earlier at equilibrium
∴ n is constant
$n = \frac{{PV}}{{RT}} = \frac{{0.4 \times 20}}{{RT}}$ … (ii)
Put equation (ii) in equation (i)
$V = \left[ {\frac{{0.4 \times 20}}{{RT}}} \right]\frac{{RT}}{{1.6}} = 5L$

Question: When an object is shot from the bottom of a long smooth inclined plane kept at an angle 60° with horizontal, it can travel a distance \[{x_1}\] along the plane. But when the inclination is decreased to 30° and the same object is shot with the same velocity, it can travel \[{x_2}\] distance. Then \[{x_1}:{\text{ }}{x_2}\] will be
Solution:
$${x_1} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g\sin {\theta _1}}}$$

$${x_2} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g\sin {\theta _2}}}$$

$$\frac{{{x_1}}}{{{x_2}}} = \frac{{\sin {\theta _2}}}{{\sin {\theta _1}}} = \frac{{\sin {{30}^ \circ }}}{{\sin {{60}^ \circ }}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$$

Question: A U tube with both ends open to the atmosphere, is partially filled with water. Oil, which is immiscible with water, is poured into one side until it stands at a distance of 10 mm above the water level on the other side. Meanwhile the water rises by 65 mm from its original level (see diagram). The density of the oil is:
Solution:
${h_{oil}}{\rho _{oil}}g = {h_{water}}{\rho _{water}}g$
$140 \times {\rho _{oil}} = 130 \times {\rho _{water}}$
${\rho _{oil}} = \frac{{13}}{{14}} \times 1000\,kg/{m^3}$
${\rho _{oil}} = 928\,kg/{m^{  3}}$

Question:
For a travelling harmonic wave
$y(x, t)=2.0 \cos 2 \pi(10 t-0.0080 x+0.35)$, where $x$ and $y$ are in cm and $t$ in s . The phase difference between oscillatory motion of two points separated by a distance of 0.5 m is:
Solution:
$y(x, t)=2.0 \cos 2 \pi(10 t-0.008 x+0.35)$
Total phase
$\phi=20 \pi-2 \pi \times 8 \times 10^{-3} x+2 \pi \times 0.35$
$\Delta \phi=k \Delta x$
$\Delta \phi=2 \pi \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \Delta x$
$=2 \pi \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times\left(\frac{100}{2}\right)$
$=8 \pi \times 10^{-1}$
$=0.8 \pi$

Question: A particle moves so that its position vector is given by \[\vec r = \cos \omega t\hat x + \sin \omega r\hat y\]. Where ω is a constant. Which of the following is true?
Options: Velocity and acceleration both are perpendicular to \[\vec r\]
Velocity and acceleration both are parallel to \[\vec r\]
Velocity is perpendicular to \[\vec r\] and acceleration is directed towards the origin
Velocity is perpendicular to \[\vec r\] and acceleration is directed away from the origin
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \vec r = \cos \omega t\,\hat x + \sin \omega t\,\hat y \\
  \vec v = - \omega \sin \omega t\,\hat x + \omega \cos \omega t\,\hat y \\
\vec a =  - {\omega ^2}\cos \omega t{\mkern 1mu} \hat x - \omega \sin \omega t{\mkern 1mu} \hat y =  - {\omega ^2}\overrightarrow r  \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\vec r.\,\vec v = 0\] 
hence \[\vec r \bot \vec v\]
\[\vec a\]  is directed towards the origin.

Question: Suppose the charge of a proton and an electron differ slightly. One of them is –e the other (e + \[\Delta e).\] If the net of electrostatic force and gravitational force between two hydrogen atoms placed at a distance d (much greater than atomic size) apart is zero, then \[\Delta e\] is of the order of [Given mass of hydrogen mh = 1.67 × 10–27 kg]

Question: Three sound waves of equal amplitudes have frequency (n - 1), n, (n + 1). They superimpose to give beats. The number of beats produced per second will be
Solution:
Net beat frequency
= LCM of individual beat frequencies
= LCM of [(n, n - 1), (n, n + 1), (n - 1, n + 1)]
= LCM of (1, 1, 2)
= 2 Hz
So, no. of beats per second = 2

Question: An ac source is connected to a capacitor C. Due to decrease in its operating frequency:
Solution:
\[{i_C} = {i_D} = \frac{{{V_O}}}{{{X_C}}}\sin \omega \,t\]
${i_C} = {i_D} = \left( {{V_O}\omega C} \right)\sin \omega \,t$
On decreasing frequency ${i_C} \downarrow $

Question: An electron falls from rest through a vertical distance h in a uniform and vertically upward directed electric field E. The direction of electric field is now reversed, keeping its magnitude the same. A proton is allowed to fall from rest in it through the same vertical distance h. The time of fall of the electron, in comparison to the time of fall of the proton is
Solution:
$h = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{eE}}{m}{t^2}$
$\therefore t = \sqrt {\frac{{2hm}}{{eE}}} $
$\therefore t \propto \sqrt m $ as ‘e’ is same for electron and proton.
∵ Electron has smaller mass so it will take smaller time.

Question: The fundamental frequency of a closed organ pipe of length 20 cm is equal to the second overtone of an organ pipe open at both the ends. The length of organ pipe open at both the ends is:
Solution:
For closed organ pipe fundamental frequency $${n_1} = \frac{v}{{4{\ell _1}}}$$
For open organ pipe fundamental frequency $${n_2} = \frac{v}{{2{\ell _2}}}$$
\[\therefore 3{n_2} = {n_1}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{3v}}{{2{\ell _2}}} = \frac{v}{{4{\ell _1}}}\therefore {\ell _2} = 6{\ell _1}\]$$ = 6{\text{ }} \times 20 = 120\;cm$$.

Question:
A mass m moves in a circle on a smooth horizontal plane with velocity v0 at a radius R0. The mass is attached to a string which passes through a smooth hole in the plane as shown.

The tension in the string is increased gradually and finally m moves in a circle of radius \[\frac{{{R_0}}}{2}\]. The final value of the kinetic energy is :
Solution:
Using conservation of angular momentum:
\[m{v_0}{R_0} = mv \times \frac{{{R_0}}}{2} \Rightarrow v = 2{v_0}\]
∴ final kinetic energy \[\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = 2mv_0^2\]

Metal displacement reactions are the ones in which more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its compound. Thus, out of the given reactions, the metal displacement reaction is:
Cr2O3 + 2Al \[\xrightarrow{\Delta }\] Al2O3 + 2Cr

Question: A particular station of All India Radio, New Delhi, broadcasts on a frequency of 1,368 KHz (kilohertz). The wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation emitted by the transmitter is: [Speed of light, c = 3 x 10<sup>8</sup> ms<sup>-1</sup>]

Question: A thin diamagnetic rod is placed vertically between the poles of an electromagnet. When the current in the electromagnet is switched on, then the diamagnetic rod is pushed up, out of the horizontal magnetic field. Hence the rod gains gravitational potential energy. The work required to do this comes from
Options: The lattice structure of the material of the rod
The induced electric field due to the changing magnetic field
Solution:
Energy of current source will be converted into potential energy of the rod.
Also,

When current source is switched on, magnetic field sets up between poles on electromagnet. Diamagnetic material, due to its tendency to move from stronger to weaker field, is thus repelled out.

Question: The radius of inner most orbit of hydrogen atom is $5.3 \times {10^{ - 11}}m$. What is the radius of third allowed orbit of hydrogen atom?

Radius of nth orbit in Hydrogen Atom
${r_n} = 0.53 \times \frac{{{n^2}}}{Z}$ Å
So, radius of third orbit
${r_3} = 0.53 \times \frac{{{{\left( 3 \right)}^2}}}{{\left( 1 \right)}}$ Å = 4.77 Å

volume ∝ moles 

nH2 : nO2 :nmethane

= 16 : 1 : 2

Question: A soap bubble, having radius of 1 mm is blown from a detergent solution having a surface tension of 2.5 × 10<sup>–2</sup> N/m. The pressure inside the bubble is equal to the pressure at a point Z<sub>0</sub> below the free surface of water in a container. Taking g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>, density of water = 10<sup>3</sup> kg/m<sup>3</sup>, the value of Z<sub>0</sub> is:
Solution:
Excess pressure \[ = \frac{{4T}}{R},\] Gauge pressure \[ = \rho g{Z_0}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  {P_0} + \frac{{4T}}{R} = {P_0} + \rho g{Z_0} \\
  {Z_0} = \frac{{4T}}{{R \times \rho g}} \\
  {Z_0} = \frac{{4 \times 2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 3}} \times 1000 \times 10}}m \\
  {Z_0} = 1cm \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Equal moles of hydrogen and oxygen gases are placed in a container with a pin-hole through which both can escape. What fraction of the oxygen escapes in the time required for one-half of the hydrogen to escape?
Solution:
Equal moles are given so partial pressure is equal (let = x)
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{{r_{{O_2}}}}}{{{r_{{H_2}}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{M_{{H_2}}}}}{{{M_{{O_2}}}}}} = \frac{{{V_{{O_2}}}}}{{{t_{{O_2}}}}} \times \frac{{{t_{{H_2}}}}}{{{V_{{H_2}}}}} = \frac{{{n_{{O_2}}}}}{{{t_{{O_2}}}}} \times \frac{{{t_{{H_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}}}}} \\
  \frac{{\frac{{{n_{{O_2}}}}}{t}}}{{\frac{{\frac{x}{2}}}{t}}} = \sqrt {\frac{2}{{32}}} = \frac{1}{4} \\
  \frac{{\frac{{{n_{{O_2}}}}}{t}}}{{\frac{{\frac{x}{2}}}{t}}} = \frac{1}{4}\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{{n_{{O_2}}}}}{x} = \frac{1}{8} \\
\end{gathered} \]
Fraction of oxygen escaped \[\frac{1}{8}\]

Question: In hydrogen spectrum, the shortest wavelength in the Balmer series is $\lambda $. The shortest wavelength in the Bracket series is:
Solution:
Shortest wavelength in Balmer series when transition of $\bar e$ from $\infty $ to $n = 2$
$\because \,\,\frac{1}{\lambda } = R{z^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{2^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{\infty ^2}}}} \right]$
$\frac{1}{\lambda } = \frac{R}{4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
Shortest wavelength is Bracket series when transition of $\bar e$ from $\infty $ to $n = 4$
$\frac{1}{{\lambda '}}' = R{\left( 1 \right)^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{4^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{\infty ^2}}}} \right] \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{\lambda '}}' = \frac{R}{{16}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 2 \right)$
Eq. (1) / Eq. (2)
$\frac{{\lambda '}}{\lambda } = \frac{R}{4} \times \frac{{16}}{R} \Rightarrow \lambda ' = 4\lambda $

(c)

Conversion of unsaturated compound into saturated compound by the addition of groups or atoms is called as addition reaction. It is electrophilic addition.

Question: A protein undergoes reversible thermal denaturation from its initial state $\mathbf{N}$ to denatured state $\mathbf{D}$ according to $\mathbf{N} \rightleftharpoons \mathbf{D}$. At $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the concentrations of both $\mathbf{N}$ and $\mathbf{D}$ are equal at equilibrium, and the standard enthalpy change of denaturation is $666 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The standard entropy change ( $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}$ in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) of the protein upon denaturation at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is closest to
Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{666}{333} \\
& =2 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: In a uniform magnetic field of 0.049T, a magnetic needle performs 20 complete oscillations in 5 seconds as shown. The moment of inertia of the needle is $9.8 \times {10^{ - 6}}kg\,{m^2}$. If the magnitude of the needle is $x \times {10^{ - 5}}A{m^2}$; then the value of ‘x’ is:

$\tau = - BM\sin \theta = I\alpha $
For small $\theta $
$\alpha = - \frac{{BM}}{I}\theta $
So, $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{I}{{BM}}} \Rightarrow M = \frac{{4{\pi ^2}I}}{{B{T^2}}} = \frac{{4{\pi ^2} \times 9.8 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{0.049 \times {{\left( {\frac{1}{4}} \right)}^2}}}$
$ = 1280{\pi ^2} \times {10^{ - 5}}$

Question: For sound waves, if the number of nodes for the $5^{\text {th }}$ harmonic of an open-ended pipe is $n$ and that for the $9^{\text {th }}$ harmonic of the same pipe with one of its ends closed is $m$, the ratio $\frac{n}{m}$ is
Solution:
For open organ pipe
→ Number of nodes are same as number of harmonics
$\therefore \quad n=5$
For closed organ pipe
→ There are only odd harmonics
For $1^{\text {st }}$ harmonic → Number of node → 1
For $3^{\text {rd }}$ harmonic → Number of node → 2
For $5^{\text {th }}$ harmonic → Number of node $\rightarrow 3$
For $7^{\text {th }}$ harmonic → Number of node $\rightarrow 4$
For $9^{\text {th }}$ harmonic → Number of node $\rightarrow 5$
$\therefore \quad m=5$
$\rightarrow \frac{n}{m}=\frac{5}{5}=1$

$\left( {{V_A} - {V_B}} \right) - L\frac{{di}}{{dt}} - 5 - iR = 0$
$\left( {{V_A} - {V_b}} \right) - 1\left( 1 \right) - 5 - 2\left( 2 \right) = 0$
${V_A} - {V_B} = 10V$

Question: An em wave is propagating in a medium with a velocity $\vec V = V\hat i$. The instantaneous oscillating electric field of this em wave is along +y axis. Then the direction of oscillating magnetic field of the em wave will be along

So, $\vec B = B\hat k$
Direction of propagation is along +z direction.

Question: The power radiated by a black body is P and it radiates maximum energy at wavelength ${\lambda _0}$. If the temperature of the black body is now changed so that it radiates maximum energy at wavelength $\frac{3}{4}{\lambda _0}$, the power radiated by it becomes nP. The value of n is
Solution:
We know,
${\lambda _{\max }}T = $ constant (Wien’s law)
So, ${\lambda _{{{\max }_1}}}{T_1} = {\lambda _{{{\max }_2}}}\,{T_2}$
$ \Rightarrow {\lambda _0}T = \frac{{3{\lambda _0}}}{4}T'$
$ \Rightarrow T' = \frac{4}{3}T$
So, $\frac{{{P_2}}}{{{P_1}}} = {\left( {\frac{{T'}}{T}} \right)^4} = {\left( {\frac{4}{3}} \right)^4} = \frac{{256}}{{81}}$

Question:

Statement II: As the size of the elements F, Cl, Br, I increase down the group, the bond strength of HF, HCl, HBr and HI decreases and so the acid strength increases.
Options: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is false.
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is true.

Both Statement I and Statement II are false.

Question: A siren emitting a sound of frequency 800 Hz moves away from an observer towards a cliff at a speed of 15 m s<sup>-1</sup>. Then, the frequency of sound that the observer hears in the echo reflected from the cliff is (Take velocity of sound in air = 330 m s<sup>-1</sup>)
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  n' = \frac{v}{{v - {v_s}}}{n_0} \\
  n' = \frac{{330}}{{330 - 15}}\left( {800} \right) \\
   = \frac{{330 \times 800}}{{315}} = 838 \,Hz \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Steam at 100<sup>o</sup>C is passed into 20 g of water at 10<sup>o</sup>C. When water acquires a temperature of 80<sup>o</sup>C, the mass of water present will be: [Take specific heat of water = 1cal g<sup>-1</sup> <sup>o</sup>C<sup>-</sup><sup>1</sup> and latent heat of steam = 540 cal g<sup>-1</sup>]
Solution:
Let m be the mass of steam than converted to the water at $${80^o}C$$

so the heat released by the m amount of water will be used to raise the temperature of $$20g$$ water 

heat lost=heat gain

$${\text{ }}540{\text{ m }} + {\text{ m}}\left( {100{\text{ }} - {\text{ }}80} \right){\text{ }} = {\text{ }}20\left( {80{\text{ }} - {\text{ }}10} \right){\text{ }}$$
$$\;m = {\text{ }}2.5\,g$$

∴ Total water at $${80^o}C$$ = $$20{\text{ }} + {\text{ }}2.5{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}22.5g$$

Question:
Water flows in a streamline motion through a horizontal pipe of circular cross-section as shown in the figure. The pressure difference of water between $P$ and $Q$ is $15 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. The area of cross-section at $P$ and $Q$ are 40 $\mathrm{cm}^{2}$ and $20 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$, respectively. The rate of flow of water through the pipe, in $\mathrm{cm}^{3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, is:
[Take density of water $=1000 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ ]
Solution:
$A_{P} V_{P}=A_{Q} V_{Q}$
$40 V_{P}=20 V_{Q}, \quad V_{Q}=2 V_{P}$
Using Bernoulli's equation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P_{P}-P_{Q}=\frac{1}{2} \rho\left(V_{Q}^{2}-V_{P}^{2}\right) \\
& 15=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^{3}\left(4 V_{P}^{2}-V_{P}^{2}\right) \\
& V_{P}=\sqrt{\frac{15}{1500}}=0.1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& V_{Q}=0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \text { Rate }=A_{P} V_{P}=40 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{3} / \mathrm{s} \\
& =4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^{3} / \mathrm{s} \\
& =4 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} / \mathrm{s} \\
& =400 \mathrm{~cm}^{3} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: One main scale division of a Vernier calliper is equal to 1 mm and the number of divisions on the Vernier scale is 10 . When both the jaws touch each other, the Vernier scale shifts to the left of zero of the main scale in such a way that $4^{\text {th }}$ Vernier division coincides with a division of the main scale. If this Vernier calliper measures the length of the wire to be 1 cm , the actual length of the wire is :
Solution:
⇒ There is negative zero error
$\Rightarrow$ Zero error $=-(10-4) \times$ L.C. $\ldots$ (i)
From ...(i)
Zero error $=-(10-4) \times 0.01=-0.06 \mathrm{~cm}$
⇒ Zero correction $=-$ Zero error
$=-(-0.06)$
$=0.06 \mathrm{~cm}$
⇒ Correct reading $=R^{\prime}$
$R^{\prime}=R+$ Zero correction
$=1+0.06$
$=1.06 \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ No option is correct

Question: A source of unknown frequency gives 4 beats per second, when sounded with a source of known frequency 250 Hz. The second harmonic of the source of unknown frequency gives five beats per second, when sounded with a source of frequency 513 Hz. The unknown frequency is
Solution:
Let the frequency of unknown source be ‘x’.
x = 250 ± 4 = 246 Hz or 254 Hz
Second Harmonic of the source, 2x = 513 ± 5 = 508 Hz or 518 Hz
∴ x = 259 or 254
⇒ x = 254 Hz

Question: In some appropriate units, time (t) and position $\left( x \right)$ relation of a moving particle is given by $t = {x^2} + x$. The acceleration of the particle is
Solution:
$t = {x^2} + x$
Derivative w.r.t $x$
$\frac{{dt}}{{dx}} = 2x + 1$
$\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{2x + 1}}$
$\therefore \boxed{v = \frac{1}{{2x + 1}}}$
Derivative w.r.t. time t
$\therefore a = \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{\left( {2x + 1} \right)}^2}}}\frac{{2dx}}{{dt}}$
$ = \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{\left( {2x + 1} \right)}^2}}}2v$
$ = \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{\left( {2x + 1} \right)}^2}}}2\frac{1}{{\left( {2x + 1} \right)}}$
$ = \frac{{ - 2}}{{{{\left( {2x + 1} \right)}^3}}}$

Question: Hydrogen atom in ground state is excited by a monochromatic radiation of λ = 975\[\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} \]. Number of spectral lines in the resulting spectrum emitted will be

⇒ n = 4
∴ Number of lines = \[ = n\frac{{\left( {n - 1} \right)}}{2} = 6\]

Question: A speeding motorcyclist sees traffic jam ahead of him. He slows down to 36 km/hour. He finds that traffic has eased and a car moving ahead of him at 18 km/hour is honking at a frequency of 1392 Hz. If the speed of sound is 343 m/s, the frequency of the honk as heard by him will be
Solution:
As Listener is moving opposite to the direction of the sound and Car also going opposite to the velocity of the sound , means listener is approaching the source and source is receding the observer hence the apparent frequency is given by is
\[f' = \left( {\frac{{V + {V_0}}}{{V + {V_s}}}} \right)f = \left( {\frac{{343 + 10}}{{343 + \left( 5 \right)}}} \right)1392 = 1412\]Hz

Question: If the initial tension on a stretched string is doubled, then the ratio of the initial and final speeds of a transverse wave along the string is :
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  v = \sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} \\
  {T_f} = 2{T_i} \\
  \frac{{{v_i}}}{{{v_f}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{T_i}}}{{{T_f}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{1}{2}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The temperature of a metallic sphere of radius $R$ is increased by a small amount $\Delta T$. If the linear coefficient of thermal expansion of the metal is $\alpha$, the approximate increase in the volume of the sphere is:
Solution:
For the metallic sphere, $\frac{\Delta R}{R}=\alpha \Delta T$
Volume of the sphere, $V=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}$
$\therefore \frac{\Delta V}{V}=3 \frac{\Delta R}{R}$
Increase in volume $=\Delta V=3\left(\frac{\Delta R}{R}\right)\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}\right)=4 \pi R^{3} \alpha \Delta T$

Question: A body cools from a temperature 3T to 2T in 10 minutes. The room temperature is T. Assume that Newton's law of cooling is applicable. The temperature of the body at the end of next 10 minutes will be
Solution:
Newton’s laws of cooling
$\frac{{{T_1} - {T_2}}}{t} = k\left( {\frac{{{T_1} + {T_2}}}{2} - T} \right)$
$\frac{{3T - 2T}}{{10}} = k\left( {\frac{{5T - 2T}}{2}} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{T}{{10}} = k\left( {\frac{{3T}}{2}} \right)$ … (i)
Suppose in next 10 min, the temperature is $$T'.$$
$\frac{{2T - T'}}{{10}} = k\left( {\frac{{2T + T'}}{2} - T} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{2T - T'}}{{10}} = k\left( {\frac{{T'}}{2}} \right)$ … (ii)
By solving (i) and (ii)
$T' = \frac{3}{2}T$

Question: Carboxylic acids have higher boiling points than aldehydes, ketones and even alcohols of comparable molecular mass. It is due to their

Solution: Due to formation of intermolecular H-bonding in carboxylic acid, association occurs. Hence boiling point increases and become more than the boiling point of aldehydes, ketones and alcohols of comparable molecular masses.

Question: Two particle A and B are moving in uniform circular motion in concentric circles of radii \[{r_A}\] and \[{r_B}\]<sub> </sub>with speed \[{v_A}\] and \[{v_B}\] respectively. Their time period of rotation is the same. The ratio of angular speed of A to that of B will be:
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {T_A} = {T_B} = T \\
  {\omega _A} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{{T_A}}} \\
  {\omega _B} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{{T_B}}} \\
  \frac{{{\omega _A}}}{{{\omega _B}}} = \frac{{{T_B}}}{{{T_A}}} = \frac{T}{T} = 1 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Two wires are made of the same material and have the same volume. The first wire has cross-sectional area A and the second wire has cross-sectional area 3A. If the length of the first wire is increased by $\Delta l$ on applying a force F, how much force is needed to stretch the second wire by the same amount?
Solution:
For wire 1,
$\Delta l = \left( {\frac{F}{{AY}}} \right)3l$ … (i)
For wire 2,
$\frac{{F'}}{{3A}} = Y\frac{{\Delta l}}{l}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta l = \left( {\frac{{F'}}{{3A\,Y}}} \right)l$ … (ii)
From equation (i) & (ii),
$\Delta l = \left( {\frac{{F}}{{AY}}} \right)3l = \left( {\frac{{F'}}{{3AY}}} \right)l \Rightarrow F' = 9F$

Question: A parallel plate capacitor made of circular plates is being charged such that the surface charge density on its plates is increasing at a constant rate with time. The magnetic field arising due to displacement current is:

Zero between the plates and non-zero outside
Constant between the plates and zero outside the plates

Non-zero everywhere with maximum at the imaginary cylindrical surface connecting peripheries of the plates
$\oint {\vec B.d\vec l} = {\mu _0}\left( {{i_C} + {\varepsilon _0}\frac{{\partial \phi }}{{\partial t}}} \right)$
$\therefore $Maxwell Ampere law
In the gap, there’s no conduction but there is displacement current due to changing electric field.
This current leads to magnetic field in the region
If increases radially with in the places, reaching maximum at the edge.

Question: The second overtone of an open organ pipe has the same frequency as the first overtone of a closed pipe L meter long. The length of the open pipe will be
Solution:
For second overtone (3rd harmonic) in open organ pipe,
$\frac{{3\lambda }}{2} = {\ell _0} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{2{\ell _0}}}{3}$
For first overtone (3rd harmonic) in closed organ pipe,
$\frac{{3\lambda }}{4} = {\ell _c} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{4{\ell _c}}}{3} = \frac{{4L}}{3}$
So, $\frac{{2{\ell _0}}}{3} = \frac{{4L}}{3} \Rightarrow {\ell _0} = 2L$

Question: The bulk modulus of a spherical object is 'B'. If it is subjected to uniform pressure 'p', the fractional decrease in radius is

$$\frac{{\Delta V}}{V} =  - \frac{p}{B}$$
$$3\frac{{\Delta r}}{r} =  - \frac{p}{B}$$
$$\frac{{\Delta r}}{r} =  - \frac{p}{{3B}}$$

-ve sign indicate that decrease in radius.

Question:
Consider a water tank shown in the figure. It has one wall at $x = L$ and can be taken to be very wide in the z direction. When filled with a liquid of surface tension S and density $\rho ,$ the liquid surface makes angle ${\theta _0}\left( {{\theta _0} < < 1} \right)$ with the $x - $axis at $x = L$. If $y\left( x \right)$ is the height of the surface then the equation for $y\left( x \right)$ is:

(take $\theta \left( x \right) = \sin \theta \left( x \right) = \tan \theta \left( x \right) = \frac{{dy}}{{dx}},g$ is the acceleration due to gravity)
Solution:
\[R = \frac{{{{\left[ {1 + {{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}^2}} \right]}^{\frac{3}{2}}}}}{{\left| {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right|}}\]
$\theta $ is very small, $\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}$ is small
$R \approx \frac{1}{{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)}}$
Laplace equation $:\Delta p = \frac{T}{R}$
$\Delta p = T\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}$
$\frac{{\rho gh}}{T} = \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}$

Question:
The slope of Arrhenius plot (lnk v/s 1/T) of first order reaction is -5 ⨉ 103 K. The value of Ea of the reaction is. Choose the correct option for your answer.
[Given R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1]
Solution:
The logarithmic form of Arrhenius equation is
\[\ln k = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}} + \ln A\]
Slope = –Ea/R ⇒ Ea = –(slope)R
Ea = 5 × 103 × 8.314 =41.57 × 103

Question:
A submarine is designed to withstand an absolute pressure of 100 atm . How deep can it go below the water surface?
(Consider the density of water $=1000 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$,
$1 \mathrm{~atm}=1 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$ and gravitational acceleration $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ )
Solution:
$P=P_{0}+\rho g h$
$\Rightarrow 100 \times 10^{5}=10^{5}+10^{3} \times 10 \times h$
$\Rightarrow 10^{7}=10^{5}+10^{4} h$
$\Rightarrow 10^{3}=10+h$
$\Rightarrow h=1000-10=990 \mathrm{~m}$

Question:
When a ruler falls vertically, 5 different persons catch it with different reaction times.
$$
\left(g=9.8 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}\right)
$$

→ There will be large distance for large reaction time

→ Descending order of distance covered
$\Rightarrow \quad S_{B}>S_{E}>S_{A}>S_{D}>S_{C}$

Question: A particle of unit mass undergoes one-dimensional motion such that its velocity varies according to ν(x) = βx - 2n, Where β and n are constants and x is the position of the particle. The acceleration of the particle as a function of x, is given by:
Solution:
ν(x) = βx - 2n
\[\alpha = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}.\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{{dv}}{{dx.}}v\]
$$\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} =  - 2n\beta {x^{2n - 1}}.\alpha  =  - 2n\beta {x^{ - 2n - 1}}.\beta {x^{ - 2n}}$$
$$ =  - 2n{\beta ^2}{x^{ - 4n - 1}}$$

Number of tetrahedral voids = 2n = 12
Number of octahedral voids = n = 6

Question: In a vernier calipers, $\left( {N + 1} \right)$ divisions of vernier scale coincide with N divisions of main scale. If 1MSD represents 0.1mm, the vernier constant (in cm) is:
Solution:
$\left( {N + 1} \right)$ of V.S.D = N div of M.S.D
1 V.S.D $ = \frac{N}{{N + 1}}M.S.D$
$L.C = 1M.S.D - 1V.S.D$
$ = 1M.S.D - \frac{N}{{N + 1}}M.S.D$
$ = \left( {\frac{{N + 1 - N}}{{N + 1}}} \right)M.S.D$
$ = \frac{1}{{N + 1}}M.S.D$
$ = \frac{{0.1}}{{N + 1}}mm$
$ = \frac{{0.01}}{{N + 1}}c.m$
$ = \frac{1}{{100\left( {N + 1} \right)}}c.m$

$\mathbf{A}$ is correct but $\mathbf{R}$ is not correct.
$\mathbf{A}$ is not correct but $\mathbf{R}$ is correct
Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are correct and $\mathbf{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$

$\mathrm{N}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}^{3}$
$\mathrm{F}: 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}^{5}$
First ionization enthalpy ( $\Delta_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{H}$ )

Due to half filled stable electronic configuration of nitrogen, it's first ionization enthalpy is higher than that of oxygen

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}^{+}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \\

$$

Question: A long solenoid of diameter 0.1 m has 2×104 turns per meter. At the center of the solenoid, a coil of 100 turns and radius 0.01 m is placed with its axis coinciding with the solenoid axis. The current in the solenoid reduces at a constant rate to 0 A from 4 A in 0.05 s. If the resistance of the coil is 10π2 Ω, the total charge flowing through the coil during this time is
Solution:
$\varepsilon = - N\frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}}$
$\left| {\frac{\varepsilon }{R}} \right| = \frac{N}{R}\frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}}$                    (as $$i = \frac{{dq}}{{dt}}$$)
$dq = \frac{N}{R}d\phi $
$$dq = \frac{{N\left( {d\phi } \right)}}{R}$$
$$dq = \frac{{d{\phi _{total}}}}{R}$$$ = \frac{{\left( {NBA} \right)}}{R}$
$$dq = \frac{{{\mu _0}ni\pi {r^2}}}{R}$$
Putting values
$$ = \frac{{4\pi  \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 2 \times {{10}^4} \times 100 \times 4 \times \pi  \times {{\left( {0.01} \right)}^2}}}{{10{\pi ^2}}} \Rightarrow dq = 32{\mkern 1mu} \mu C$$

Question:
A small block slides down on a smooth inclined plane, starting from rest at time t = 0. Let Sn be the distance travelled by the block in the interval t = n – 1 to t = n,
Then, the ratio  $\frac{{{S_n}}}{{{S_n} + 1}}$ is:
Solution:
$$\eqalign{
  & {S_{{n^{th}}}} = u + \frac{a}{2}\left[ {2n - 1} \right] \cr
  & u = 0 \cr
  & a = g\sin \theta \cr
  & {S_{{n^{th}}}} = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{2}\left[ {2n - 1} \right] \cr
  & {S_{{{\left( {n + 1} \right)}^{th}}}} = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{2}\left[ {2n + 1} \right] \cr
  & \frac{{{S_n}}}{{{S_{\left( {n + 1} \right)}}}} = \frac{{2n - 1}}{{2n + 1}} \cr} $$
