Question: For a plane electromagnetic wave propagating in x - direction, which one of the following combination gives the correct possible directions for electric field (E) and magnetic field (B) respectively.
Solution:
For direction
$$\eqalign{
  & \vec E \times \vec B = \vec C \cr
  & 1.\,\,\left( {\hat j + \hat k} \right) \times \left( { - \hat j - \hat k} \right) = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(wrong) \cr
  & 2.\,\,\left( {\hat j \times \hat k} \right) \times \left( { - \hat j \times \hat k} \right) = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(wrong) \cr
  & 3.\,\,\left( {\hat j + \hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat j + \hat k} \right) = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(wrong) \cr
  & 4.\,\,\left( { - \hat j + \hat k} \right) \times \left( { - \hat j - \hat k} \right) = \hat i + \hat i = 2\hat i\,(correct) \cr} $$

Question: Preeti reached the metro station and found that the escalator was not working. She walked up the stationary escalator in time t1. On other day, if she remains stationary on the moving escalator, then the escalator takes her up in time t2. The time taken by her to walk up on the moving escalator will be
Solution:
Velocity of girl w.r.t. elevator $ = \frac{d}{{{t_1}}} = {v_{ge}}$
Velocity of elevator w.r.t. ground ${v_{eG}} = \frac{d}{{{t_2}}}$ then
velocity of girl w.r.t. ground
${\vec v_{gG}} = {\vec v_{ge}} + {\vec v_{eG}}$
i.e., ${v_{gG}} = {v_{ge}} + {v_{eG}}$                                
$\frac{d}{t} = \frac{d}{{{t_1}}} + \frac{d}{{{t_2}}}$
$\frac{1}{t} = \frac{1}{{{t_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{t_2}}}$
$t = \frac{{{t_1}{t_2}}}{{\left( {{t_1} + {t_2}} \right)}}$

Question: In a vernier calliper, 20 VSD coincide with 16 MSD (each division of length 1 mm ). The least count of the vernier callipers is:
Solution:
Least count of vernier callipers
L.C. $=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD}$.......(i)
$$
\begin{equation*}
20 \mathrm{VSD}=16 \mathrm{MSD}
\end{equation*}
$$
$1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{16}{20} \mathrm{MSD}$
From (i)
L.C. $=1 \mathrm{MSD}-\frac{16}{20} \mathrm{MSD}$
L.C. $=\frac{4}{20} \mathrm{MSD}$
L.C. $=\frac{1}{5} \mathrm{~mm}=0.2 \mathrm{~mm}$
L.C. $=0.02 \mathrm{~cm}$

Question: The electric field in a certain region is acting radially outward and is given by $$E{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}Ar$$ where r is distance from centre of sphere. A charge contained in a sphere of radius \[\alpha \] centered at the origin of the field, will be given by:
Solution:
\[\oint {\overrightarrow E .} \overrightarrow {ds} = \,E\,4\pi {\alpha ^2} = A.\,\alpha 4\pi {\alpha ^2} = 4\pi \,A{\alpha ^3}\]
\[\oint {\overrightarrow E .\,\overrightarrow {ds} = \frac{{{Q_{en}}}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}\,\,\,\,\therefore {Q_{en}} = } 4\pi {\varepsilon _0}\,A\,{\alpha ^3}\]

Question:
A spherical conductor of radius 10 cm has a charge of \[3.2{\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{ - {\mathbf{7}}}}\] C distributed uniformly, what is the magnitude of electric field at a point 15 cm from the centre of the sphere ?
\[\left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi { \in _{\mathbf{0}}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}9{\mathbf{ \times }}{{10}^{\mathbf{9}}}\,N{m^{\mathbf{2}}}/{C^{\mathbf{2}}}} \right)\]
Solution:
\[E{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{kQ}}{{{r^2}}} = \;\frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 3.2 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{15 \times 15 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}\]
\[E{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{3.2}}{{25}}{\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{\mathbf{6}}}{\mathbf{ = }}0.128{\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{\mathbf{6}}}\]
\[E{\mathbf{ = }}1.28{\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{\mathbf{5}}}\,N/C\]

Question:
Which one of the following conditions will favour maximum formation of the product in the reaction,
${A_2}\left( g \right) + {B_2}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons {x_2}\left( g \right){\Delta _r}H = - XkJ$?
Solution:
${A_2}\left( g \right) + {B_2}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons {x_2}\left( g \right)\Delta H = - xkJ$
On increasing pressure equilibrium shifts in a direction where pressure decreases i.e.
forward direction.
On decreasing temperature, equilibrium shifts in exothermic direction i.e., forward direction.
So, high pressure and low temperature favours maximum formation of product.

Question: A particle of mass 10 g moves along a circle of radius 6.4 cm with a constant tangential acceleration. What is the magnitude of this acceleration if the kinetic energy of the particle becomes equal to 8 × 10<sup>−4</sup>J by the end of the second revolution after the beginning of the motion?
Solution:
\[\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = E\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{10}}{{1000}}} \right){v^2} = 8 \times {10^{ - 4}}\]
⇒ v2 = 16 × 10−2
⇒ v = 4 × 10−1 = 0.4 m/s
Now,
V2= u2 + 2ats                  (s = 4π R)
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{16}}{{100}} = {0^2} + 2{a_t}\left( {4 \times \frac{{22}}{7} \times \frac{{6.4}}{{100}}} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,{a_t} = \frac{{16}}{{100}} \times \frac{{7 \times 100}}{{8 \times 22 \times 6.4}} = 0.1\,\,m/{s^2} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A hollow metal sphere of radius R is uniformly charged. The electric field due to the sphere at a distance r from the center:
Options: zero as r increases for r < R, decreases as r increases for r > R
zero as r increases for r < R, increases as r increases for r > R
decreases as r increases for r < R and for r > R
increases as r increases for r < R and for r > R
Solution:
Charge Q will be distributed over the surface of hollow metal sphere.
(i) For r < R (inside)
By Gauss law, \[\oint {{{\bar E}_{in}}.\overline {dS} } = \frac{{{q_{en}}}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,{E_{in}} = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because \,{q_{en}} = 0} \right)\]
(ii) For r > R (outside)

\[\begin{gathered}
  \oint {{{\bar E}_0}.\overline {dS} } = \frac{{{q_{en}}}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \\
  Here,\,\,{q_{en}} = Q\left( {\because {q_{en}} = Q} \right) \\
  \therefore \,{E_0}4\pi {r^2} = \frac{Q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \\
  \therefore {E_0} \propto \frac{1}{{{r^2}}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Light with an energy flux of 25 × 10<sup>4</sup> Wm<sup>− 2</sup> falls on a perfectly reflecting surface at normal incidence. If the surface area is 15 cm<sup>2</sup> , the average force exerted on the surface is

\[F = PA = \left( {\frac{{2I}}{c}} \right)A = \frac{{2 \times 25 \times {{10}^4} \times \left( {15 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}\]
= 25 × 10− 7 = 2.5 × 10− 6 N

Question: The velocity of a small ball of mass M and density d, when dropped in a container filled with glycerine becomes constant after some time. If the density of glycerine is $\frac{d}{2},$ then the viscous force acting on the ball will be :
Solution:
Viscous force = weight - buoyant force
$$\eqalign{
  & = V{d}g - V{d_2}g \cr
  & = V{d}g - V\frac{d}{2}g \cr
  & = \frac{{Vdg}}{2} = \frac{{Mg}}{2} \cr} $$

Question:
Using the Gibbs energy change, ΔG0 = + 63.3KJ, for the following reaction,
\[A{g_2}C{O_3}\left( s \right) \rightleftharpoons 2A{g^ + }\left( {aq} \right) + CO_3^{2 - }\left( {aq} \right)\]

Solution:

Question: A balloon is made of a material of surface tension S and its inflation outlet (from where gas is filled in it) has small area A. It is filled with a gas of density $\rho $ and takes a spherical shape of radius R. When the gas is allowed to flow freely out of it, its radius r changes from R to 0 (zero) in time T. If the speed $v\left( r \right)$ of gas coming out of the balloon depends on r as ${r^a}$ and $T \propto {S^\alpha }{A^\beta }{\rho ^\gamma }{R^\delta }$ then
Solution:
$T \propto {S^\alpha }{A^\beta }{\rho ^r}{R^\delta }$
$\left[ T \right] = {\left[ {M{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^\alpha }{\left[ {{L^2}} \right]^\beta }{\left[ {M{L^{ - 3}}} \right]^\gamma }\left[ {{L^\delta }} \right]$
$\left[ T \right] = \left[ {{M^{\alpha + \gamma }}{L^{2\beta - 3\gamma + \delta }}{T^{ - 2\alpha }}} \right]$
Company powers
$ - 2\alpha = 1\,\,....\left( 1 \right) \Rightarrow \alpha = - \frac{1}{2}$
$\alpha + \gamma = 0\,\,..\left( 2 \right)\, \Rightarrow \gamma = \frac{1}{2}$
$2\beta - 3\gamma + \delta = 0\,\,....\left( 3 \right)$
$2\beta - \frac{3}{2} + \delta = 0$
$2\beta = \frac{3}{2} - \delta $
If $\delta = \frac{7}{2}$
$2\beta = \frac{3}{2} - \frac{7}{2} = \frac{{ - 4}}{2} = - 2$
$\beta = - 1$
\[a = - \frac{1}{2},\alpha = - \frac{1}{2},\beta = - 1,\gamma = - \frac{1}{2},\delta = \frac{7}{2}\]

Question: If \[{\theta _1}\] and \[{\theta _2}\] be the apparent angles of dip observed in two vertical planes at right angles to each other, then the true angle of dip \[\theta \] is given by
Solution:
Let BH and BV be the horizontal and vertical components of earth's magnetic field vector B. Since $$\theta $$ is the true angle of dip

$$\therefore \,\,\tan \theta  = \frac{{{B_V}}}{{{B_H}}}\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\cot \theta  = \frac{{{B_H}}}{{{B_V}}}$$       ...(i)

Suppose planes 1 and 2 are mutually perpendicular plane and respectively make angles $$\theta \,and\,(90^\circ  - \theta )$$ with the magnetic meridian. The vertical component of earth's magnetic field remain same in the two planes but the effective horizontal components in the planes will be

$${B_1} = {B_H}\cos \theta \,\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{B_2} = {B_H}\sin \theta $$

The angles of dip $${\theta _1}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{\theta _2}$$ in the two planes are given by

$$\tan {\theta _1} = \frac{{{B_V}}}{{{B_1}}}$$

$$\tan {\theta _1} = \frac{{{B_V}}}{{{B_H}\cos \theta }}$$

$${\text{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \cot {\theta _1} = \frac{{{B_H}\cos \theta }}{{{B_V}}}$$              ...(ii)

Similarly, $$\cot {\theta _2} = \frac{{{B_H}\sin \theta }}{{{B_V}}}$$          ..(iii)

From eqns. (ii) and (iii)

$${\cot ^2}{\theta _1} + {\cot ^2}{\theta _2} = \frac{{{B_H}^2}}{{{B_V}^2}}$$                                    [from eqn. (i)]

$$\therefore \,\,{\cot ^2}{\theta _1} + {\cot ^2}{\theta _2} = {\cot ^2}\theta $$

Question: The unit of thermal conductivity is:
Solution:
The heat current related to difference of temperature across the length \[l\] of a conductor of area A is
\[\frac{{dH}}{{dt}} = \frac{{KA}}{l}\Delta T\] (K = coefficient of thermal conductivity)
\[\therefore \,K = \frac{{l\,dH}}{{A\,dt\,\Delta T}}\]
Unit of K = Wm-1 K-1

Question:
The equilibrium constants of the following are:
N2 + 3H2 \[ \rightleftharpoons \] 2NH3 K1

N2 + O2 \[ \rightleftharpoons \] 2NO K2

\[{H_2} + \frac{1}{2}{O_2} \to {H_2}O\] K3
The equilibrium constant (K) of the reaction

\[2N{H_3} + \frac{5}{2}{O_2} \rightleftharpoons 2NO + 3{H_2}O\] will be
Solution:
(I) ${N_2} + 3{H_2} \rightleftharpoons 2N{H_3};\,{K_1} = \frac{{{{\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}^2}}}{{\left[ {{N_2}} \right]{{\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}^3}}}$
(II) ${N_2} + {O_2} \rightleftharpoons 2NO;\,{K_2} = \frac{{{{\left[ {NO} \right]}^2}}}{{\left[ {{N_2}} \right]\left[ {{O_2}} \right]}}$
(III) ${H_2} + \frac{1}{2}{O_2} \rightleftharpoons {H_2}O;\,{K_3} = \frac{{{{\left[ {{H_2}O} \right]}}}}{{\left[ {{H_2}} \right]{{\left[ {{O_2}} \right]}^{1/2}}}}$
(II + 3 × III − I) will give
$2N{H_3} + \frac{5}{2}{O_2} \rightleftharpoons 2NO + 3{H_2}O$
$\therefore K = {K_2} \times K_3^3/{K_1}$

Question:
Two identical inductors are connected in two different configurations $P$ and $Q$, where a time varying current $I(t)$ is flowing, as shown in the figure. The induced emf between points $a$ and $b$ for configuration $P$ is $E_{P}$ and that for configuration $Q$ is $E_{Q}$. The ratio $E_{P} / E_{Q}$ is:
[Neglect the effect of mutual inductance.]
Solution:
In figure ( $P$ )
$$
\begin{equation*}
E_{P}=E_{a b}=-\frac{L d I(t)}{d t} \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$

In figure (Q)
$$

$$

$$

Question: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance 20 $\mu $F is being charged by a voltage source whose potential is changing at the rate of 3 V/s. The conduction current through the connecting wires and the displacement current through the plates of the capacitor would be respectively.
Solution:
Capacitance of capacitor \[C = 20\mu F\]
= 20 × 10-6 F
Rate of change of potential \[\left( {\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}} \right) = 3v/s\]
q = CV
\[\frac{{dq}}{{dt}} = C\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  {i_c} = 20 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 3 \\
   = 60 \times {10^{ - 6}}A = 60\mu A \\
\end{gathered} \]
As we know that \[{i_d} = {i_c} = 60\mu A\] .

Question: Following figures show the arrangement of bar magnets in different configurations. Each magnet has magnetic dipole moment \[\overrightarrow M \]. Which configuration has highest net magnetic dipole moment?
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  M = \sqrt {M_1^2 + M_2^2 + 2{M_1}{M_2}\cos \theta } \\
  {\text{We}}\,{\text{have}}\,{M_1} = {M_2} = M\left( {say} \right) \\
  {\text{For}}\,\theta = {90^o} \\
  {M_{net}} = M\sqrt 2 \\
  {\text{For}}\,\theta = {180^o} \\
  {M_{net}} = 0 \\
  {\text{For}}\,\theta = {30^o} \\
  {M_{net}} = M\sqrt {2 + \sqrt 3 } \\
  {\text{For}}\,\theta = {60^o} \\
  {M_{net}} = M\sqrt 3 \\

Lower is the θ, Higher is the M

Question: Three identical heat-conducting rods are connected in series as shown in the figure. The rods on the sides have thermal conductivity 2K, while the one in the middle has thermal conductivity K. The left end of the combination is maintained at a temperature 3T and the right end at T. The rods are thermally insulated from the outside. In steady state, the temperature at the left junction is ${T_1}$ and that at the right junction is ${T_2}$. The ratio $\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}}$ is
Solution:
$Q = \frac{{kA\Delta T}}{L}$
$Q = \frac{{2kA}}{L}\left( {3T - {T_1}} \right) = \frac{{kA}}{L}\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right) = \frac{{2kA}}{L}\left( {{T_2} - T} \right)$
$\therefore 2\left( {3T - {T_1}} \right) = \left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right) = 2\left( {{T_2} - T} \right)$= constant
$\therefore \boxed{6T - 2{T_1} = C}\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
$\boxed{{T_1} - {T_2} = C}\,\,\,....\left( 2 \right)$
$\boxed{2{T_2} - T = C}\,\,\,....\left( 3 \right)$
$\boxed{2{T_1} - 2{T_2} = 2C}\,\,\,....\left( 4 \right)$
And (1) & (4)
$\boxed{6T - 2{T_2} = 3C}\,\,\,\,..\left( 5 \right)$
$\boxed{2{T_2} - 2T = C}$ from (3)
Add above $4T = 4C$
$\boxed{T = C}$
From (1), $6T - 2{T_1} = C$
$6C - 2{T_1} = C$
$5C = 2{T_1}$
$\boxed{\frac{{5C}}{2} = {T_1}}$
From 3, $2{T_2} - 2T = C$
$2{T_2} - 2C = C$
$2{T_2} = 3C$
$\boxed{{T_2} = \frac{{3C}}{2}}$
$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \frac{{\frac{{5C}}{2}}}{{\frac{{3C}}{2}}} = \frac{5}{3}$.

Question: A particle moving in a circle of radius R with a uniform speed takes a time T to complete one revolution. I this particle were projected with the same speed at an angle ‘𝜽’ to the horizontal, the maximum height attained by it equals 4R. The angle of projection, 𝜽, is then given by:
Solution:
$$\eqalign{
  & T = \frac{{2\pi R}}{v}\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,v = \frac{{2\pi R}}{T} \cr
  & H = \frac{{{v^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}} \cr
  & H = R \cr
  & 4R = {\left( {\frac{{2\pi R}}{T}} \right)^2}\frac{{{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}} \cr
  & \sqrt {\frac{{2g}}{R}{{\left( {\frac{T}{\pi }} \right)}^2}} = \sin \theta = \sqrt {\frac{{2g{T^2}}}{{{\pi ^2}R}}} \cr} $$

Question: A metallic bar of Young's modulus, $0.5 \times {10^{11}}\;{\text{N}}{{\text{m}}^{ - 2}}$ and coefficient of linear thermal expansion $${10^{ - 5}}{\,^{\text{o}}}{{\text{C}}^{ - 1}}$$. Length 1m and area of cross-section ${10^{ - 3}}{m^2}$ is heated from \[0^\circ C\] to \[100^\circ C\] without expansion or bending. The compressive force developed in it is:
At a point A on the earth’s surface the angle of dip $\delta  =  + 25^\circ $. At a point B on the earth’s surface the angle of dip $\delta  =  - 25^\circ $. We can interpret that:
Options: A is located in the southern hemisphere and B is located in the northern hemisphere.
A is located in the northern hemisphere and B is located in the southern hemisphere.
A and B are both located in the southern hemisphere.
A and B are both located in the northern hemisphere.
Solution:
Angle of dip is the angle between earth's resultant magnetic field from horizontal. Dip is zero at equator and positive in northern hemisphere.
 
In southern hemisphere dip angle is considered as negative.

Question: The correct sequence of bond enthalpy of ‘C-X’ bond is
Options: CH3−F < CH3 − Cl > CH3 − Br > CH3 − I

CH3−F < CH3 − Cl < CH3 − Br < CH3 − I
CH3−F > CH3 − Cl > CH3 − Br > CH3 − I

Due to high bond length C-I has less bond strength and has less bond dissociation enthalpy.
Because of short bond length C-F has high bond dissociation enthalpy so that increasing order of bond dissociation enthalpies.

Question: A student measured the diameter of a small steel ball using a screw gauge of least count 0.001 cm. The main scale reading is 5 mm and zero of circular scale division coincides with 25 divisions above the reference level. If screw gauge has a zero error of -0.004 cm, the correct diameter of the ball is
Solution:
Diameter of the ball
= MSR + CSR × (Least count) – Zero error
= 0.5 cm + 25 × 0.001 − (−0.004)
= 0.5 + 0.025 + 0.004
= 0.529 cm

Question: The ratio of wavelengths of the last line of Balmer series and the last line of Lyman series is
Solution:
for last line of Balmer series
$\frac{1}{{{\lambda _b}}} = R\left[ {\frac{1}{{{2^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{\infty ^2}}}} \right]$
${\lambda _b} = \frac{4}{R}$
For last line of Lyman series
$\frac{1}{{{\lambda _l}}} = R\left[ {\frac{1}{{{1^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{\infty ^2}}}} \right]$
${\lambda _l} = \frac{1}{R}$
$\frac{{{\lambda _b}}}{{{\lambda _l}}} = \frac{{\frac{4}{R}}}{{\frac{1}{R}}}$
$\frac{{{\lambda _b}}}{{{\lambda _l}}} = 4$

Question:
For the redox reaction
$MnO_4^ - + {C_2}O_4^{2 - } + {H^ + } \to M{n^{2 + }}+C\,{O_2} + {H_2}O$
The correct coefficients of the reactants for the balanced equation are
Solution:
$n - $ factor of $MnO_4^ - \Rightarrow 5$
$n - $ factor of ${C_2}O_4^{2 - } \Rightarrow 2$
Ratio of n-factors of $MnO_4^ - $ and ${C_2}O_4^{2 - }$ is 5:2
So, molar ratio in balanced reaction is 2 : 5
∴ The balanced equation is
$2MnO_4^ - + 5{C_2}O_4^{2 - } + 16{H^ + } \to 2M{n^{2 + }} + 10C{O_2} + 8{H_2}O$

Question: A certain number of spherical drops of a liquid of radius ‘r’ coalesce to form a single drop of radius ‘R’ and volume ‘V’. If ‘T’ is the surface tension of the liquid, then
Options: Energy is neither released nor absorbed
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  S{E_i} = T4{\pi ^2}n = T\left( {\frac{{3V}}{r}} \right) \\
  S{E_f} = T4\pi {R^2} = T\frac{{3V}}{R} \\
  \Delta E = S{E_f} - S{E_i} = T3V\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{r}} \right) \\
   = 3TV\left[ {\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{r}} \right]\,\,\, \\
  {\text{This}}\,{\text{amount}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{energy}}\,{\text{is}}\,{\text{released}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Name the gas that can readily decolourise acidified KMnO<sub>4</sub> solution

Question: The condition under which a microwave oven heats up a food item containing water molecules most efficiently is
Options: The frequency of the microwaves must match the resonant frequency of the water molecules
The frequency of the microwaves has no relation with natural frequency of water molecules.
Microwaves are heat waves, so always produce heating
Infra-red waves produce heating in a microwave oven
Solution: In the presence of microwaves, the water molecules oscillate in the electric field of microwaves. This results in the generation of heat. The amplitude of oscillation will be maximum when the frequency of the microwaves matches the resonant frequency of water molecules. Thus, for effective working, the microwaves must operate near the natural frequency of vibration of the water molecules.

a carbonyl compound with a hydrogen atom on its alpha-carbon rapidly equilibrates with its corresponding enol and this process is known as carbonyl cation.

Question: A point charge $Q$ is placed inside a cavity within a solid isolated conducting sphere. Consider points $A, B$ and $C$ as shown in the figure, where the magnitudes of the electric fields are $E_{A}, E_{B}, E_{C}$, respectively. The points $B$ and $C$ are at the same distance from the center of the solid sphere. The correct option is :

Question: The fundamental frequency in an open organ pipe is equal to the third harmonic of a closed organ pipe. If the length of the closed organ pipe is 20 cm, the length of the open organ pipe is
Solution:
For closed organ, pipe third harmonic $ = \frac{{3v}}{{4l}}$
For open organ pipe, fundamental frequency $ = \frac{v}{{2l'}}$
Given,
$\frac{{3v}}{{4l}} = \frac{v}{{2l'}}$
$ \Rightarrow l' = \frac{{4l}}{{3 \times 2}} = \frac{{2l}}{3}$
$ = \frac{{2 \times 20}}{3} = 13.33cm$

Question: A electric dipole is placed at an angle of $30^\circ $ with an electric field of intensity $2 \times {10^5}N{C^{ - 1}}$. It experiences a torque equal to 4 Nm. Calculate the magnitude of charge on the dipole, if the dipole length is 2 cm.
Solution:
$\tau $ on a dipole \[ = \vec p \times \vec E\]
$\tau = pE\sin \theta $
$4 = q \times l \times E \times \sin 30^\circ $
$4 = q \times 2 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times 2 \times {10^5} \times \frac{1}{2}$
$q = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}$
$q = 2\,\,mC$

Question: The correct order of atomic radii in group 13 elements is
Solution:
ElementsBGaAllnTl
Atomic radii (pm)85135143167170
On moving down the group the atomic radius of Ga is slightly lower than that of Al. This is due to the presence of d - electrons in Ga which do not shield the nucleus effectively. As a result, the electrons in Ga experience greater force of attraction by the nucleus than in Al and hence the atomic radius of Ga 135 pm is slightly less than that of Al 143 pm. Thus, the increasing order of atomic radii of group 13 elements is B < Ga < Al < In < Tl.

Question: A stone falls freely under gravity. It covers distances h1 , h2 and h3 in the first 5 second, the next 5 second and the next 5 second respectively. The relation between h1 , h2 and h3 is
Solution:
Distance covered in ﬁrst 5 sec
\[{h_1} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}g{(5)^2}\]
\[{h_1} = \frac{{25g}}{2}\]
Distance covered in first 10 sec
\[{s_2} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}g {(10)^2} = \frac{{100g }}{2}\]........(1)
So distance covered in second 5 sec
\[{h_2} = {s_2} - {h_1} = \frac{{100g }}{2} - \frac{{25g}}{2} = \frac{{75g}}{2}\]…… (2)
distance covered in first 15 sec
\[{s_3} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} g{(15)^2} = \frac{{225 g}}{2}\]
So distance covered in last 5 sec
\[{h_3} = {s_3} - {s_2} = \frac{{225g }}{2} - \frac{{100g }}{2} = \frac{{125g }}{2}\]………..(3)
Using (1), (2) ,(3) equation
\[\frac{{{h_1}}}{{\frac{{25g }}{2}}} = \frac{{{h_2}}}{{\frac{{75g }}{2}}} = \frac{{{h_3}}}{{\frac{{125g }}{2}}}\]
\[{h_1} = \frac{{{h_2}}}{3} = \frac{{{h_3}}}{5}\]

Question: Three liquids of densities ρ <sub>1</sub>, ρ <sub>2</sub> and ρ <sub>3</sub> (with ρ <sub>1</sub> > ρ <sub>2</sub> > ρ <sub>3</sub>), having the same value of surface tension T, rise to the same height in three identical capillaries. The angles of contact θ <sub>1</sub>, θ <sub>2</sub> and θ <sub>3</sub> obey
Solution:
$h = \frac{{2T\cos \theta }}{{\rho gr}}$
As r, h, T are same
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\cos \theta }}{\rho }$= constant
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\cos {\theta _1}}}{{{\rho _1}}} = \frac{{\cos {\theta _2}}}{{{\rho _2}}} = \frac{{\cos {\theta _3}}}{{{\rho _3}}}$
As ${\rho _1} > {\rho _2} > {\rho _3}$
$ \Rightarrow \cos {\theta _1} > \cos {\theta _2} > \cos {\theta _3}$
$ \Rightarrow {\theta _1} < {\theta _2} < {\theta _3}$
As water rises$ \Rightarrow \theta $ must be acute
So, $0 \leqslant {\theta _1} < {\theta _2} < {\theta _3} < \frac{\pi }{2}$

Question: Consider a long solenoid of length $I$ and radius $r$. If $n$ is the number of turns per unit length and $\mu_{0}$ is the permeability of free space, the inductance of the solenoid is :
Solution:
Self inductance of solenoid
$$
\begin{aligned}
& L=\frac{N \phi}{i} \\
& L=\frac{N B A}{i} \\
& L=\frac{N \mu_{0} n i A}{i}\left[n=\frac{N}{l}\right] \\
& L=N \mu_{0} n A \\
& L=\left(\frac{N}{I}\right) \mu_{0} n A I \\
& L=\mu_{0} n^{2} A I \\
& L=\mu_{0} n^{2} \pi r^{2} I\left[\because A=\pi r^{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

A series LCR circuit is connected to an AC voltage source. When L is removed from the circuit, the phase difference between current and voltage is \[\frac{\pi }{3}\]. If instead C is removed from the circuit, the phase difference is again \[\frac{\pi }{3}\] between current and voltage. The power factor of the circuit is:
Solution:
When inductor alone is removed,
\[ \theta {\mathbf{ = }}\frac{\pi }{3}\]
\[\tan  \theta {\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{X_{\mathbf{C}}}}}{R}\]
\[\tan \frac{\pi }{3}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{X_{\mathbf{C}}}}}{R}{\mathbf{ = }}\sqrt 3 \]
\[{X_{\mathbf{c}}}{\mathbf{ = }}R\sqrt 3 \]

When capacitor alone is removed,
\[\tan  \phi_c {\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{X_{\mathbf{L}}}}}{R}\]

\[\tan \frac{\pi }{3}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{X_{\mathbf{L}}}}}{R}\]
Thus, for the original circuit,
\[\sqrt 3 {\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{X_{\mathbf{L}}}}}{R}\]
\[{X_{\mathbf{L}}}{\mathbf{ = }}R\sqrt 3 {\mathbf{ = }}{X_{\mathbf{C}}}\]
\[{X_{\mathbf{L}}}{\mathbf{ = }}{X_{\mathbf{C}}} \Rightarrow (Z{\mathbf{ = }}R)\]
power factor \[{\mathbf{ = }}\cos  \phi {\mathbf{ = }}\frac{R}{Z}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{R}{R}{\mathbf{ = }}1\]

Question: To an ac power supply of 220V at 50 Hz, a resistor of$20\Omega ,$ a capacitor of reactance $25\Omega $ and an inductor of reactance $45\Omega $ are connected in series. The corresponding current in the circuit and the phase angle between the current and the voltage is, respectively -
Solution:
V = 200 V, f = 50 Hz
$R = 20\Omega ,{X_C} = 25\Omega ,{X_L} = 45\Omega $
$I = \frac{V}{Z}$
$Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {X^2}} $
$X = {X_L} - {X_C}$
$X = 45 - 25 = 20$
$Z = \sqrt {{{20}^2} + {{20}^2}} = \sqrt {800} $
$Z = 20\sqrt 2 $
$I = \frac{V}{Z} = \frac{{220}}{{20\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{11}}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
$I = 7.8$

$\cos \theta = \frac{R}{Z} = \frac{{20}}{{20\sqrt 2 }}$
$\cos \theta = \cos 45$
$\theta = 45^\circ $

$\tan \phi = \frac{{{V_L} - {V_C}}}{{{V_R}}} = \frac{{100 - 40}}{{80}} = \frac{3}{4}$ or $\phi = 37^\circ $
Power factor$ = \cos \phi = \cos 37^\circ = \frac{4}{5}$ or 0.8

Question: The ratio of the specific heats \[\frac{{{C_p}}}{{C_v}} = \gamma \] in terms of degree of freedom (n) is given by:
Solution:
For a monatomic gas
$${C_V} = \frac{3}{2}R{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ;{C_P} = \frac{5}{2}R{\mkern 1mu} ;\gamma  = \frac{{{C_P}}}{{{C_V}}} = \frac{5}{3}$$ 
For a diatomic gas
$${C_V} = \frac{5}{2}R{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ;{C_P} = \frac{7}{2}R{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ;\gamma  = \frac{{{C_P}}}{{{C_V}}} = \frac{7}{5}$$
For a triatomic gas
$${C_V} = 3R{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ;{C_P} = 4R{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ;\gamma  = \frac{{{C_P}}}{{{C_V}}} = \frac{4}{3}$$

This fits into the pattern\[\left( {1 + \frac{2}{n}} \right)\], where n is the number of the degree of freedom.

Question: In an ideal transformer, the turns ratio is $\frac{{{N_p}}}{{{N_s}}} = \frac{1}{2}$. The ratio ${V_s}:{V_p}$ is equal to (the symbols carry their usual meaning):

(Given : $L=10 \mathrm{mH}, C=25 \mu \mathrm{~F}, R=100 \Omega$ )
Solution:
Angular frequency of AC source, $\omega=2 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
Inductive reactance, $X_{L}=\omega L=\left(2 \times 10^{3}\right) \times\left(10 \times 10^{-3}\right)=20 \Omega$
Capacitive reactance, $X_{C}=\frac{1}{\omega C}=\frac{1}{\left(2 \times 10^{3}\right) \times\left(25 \times 10^{-6}\right)}=20 \Omega$
Impedance $Z$ for circuit $=R\left(\because X_{L}=X_{C}\right.$ and circuit is at resonance $)$
∴ current amplitude, $i_{0}=\frac{V_{0}}{Z}=\frac{V_{0}}{R}=\frac{220}{100}=2.2 \mathrm{~A}$

R and C should be small and L should be high.

Question: The peak value of an alternating current is 5 A and frequency is 60 Hz . How long will the current, starting from zero, take to reach the peak value ?
Solution:
Alternating current,
$i=i_{\text {peak }} \sin (\omega t)$
where $\omega=2 \pi f=2 \pi \times 60=120 \pi \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
$\Rightarrow \quad i=5 \sin (120 \pi t)$
$\Rightarrow \quad 5=5 \sin (120 \pi t)$
$\Rightarrow \sin (120 \pi t)=1=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
$\therefore \quad 120 \pi t=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\Rightarrow t=\frac{1}{240} \mathrm{~s}$

Question: A small signal voltage V(t) = V<sub>0</sub> sin \[\omega \]t is applied across an ideal capacitor C
Options: Current I(t), lags voltage V(t) by 90<sup>o</sup>
Over a full cycle the capacitor C does not consume any energy from the voltage source.
Current I(t), is in phase with voltage V(t)
Current I(t), leads voltage V(t) by 180<sup>o</sup>
Solution:
Power = Vrms. Irms cos ϕ
as cos ϕ = 0 (Because ϕ = 90o )
$\therefore $ Power consumed = 0
(in one complete cycle)

Question: A 12 V, 60 W lamp is connected to the secondary of a step down transformer, whose primary is connected to ac mains of 220 V. Assuming the transformer to be ideal, what is the current in the primary winding ?
Solution:
${V_S}{I_S} = {V_P}{I_P}$ (ideal Transformer)
$ \Rightarrow {P_{{\text{out}}}} = {P_{{\text{in}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow 60 = 220 \times {I_P}$
${I_P} = \frac{{60}}{{220}} = 0.27\,A$

Question: The peak voltage of the ac source is equal to :
Options: the value of voltage supplied to the circuit
the rms value of the ac source
\[\sqrt 2 \] times the rms value of the ac source
\[1/\sqrt 2 \]times the rms value of the ac source
Solution:
${V_{rms}} = \frac{{{V_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }} \Rightarrow {V_0} = \sqrt 2 {V_{rms}}$
Peak voltage of ac = $\sqrt 2 $times the rms value of ac source.

Question: A 40 \[\mu F\]capacitor is connected to a 200 V, 50 Hz ac supply. The rms value of the current in the circuit is, nearly:
Solution:
\[{I_{{\bf{rms}}}} = \frac{{{V_{{\bf{rms}}}}}}{Z} = \frac{{200}}{{{X_{\bf{C}}}}} = 200.\;{\mkern 1mu} 2\pi f.C\]
\[{\mathbf{ = }}200{\mathbf{ \times }}2{\mathbf{ \times }}3.14{\mathbf{ \times }}50{\mathbf{ \times }}40{\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{ - {\mathbf{6}}}}\]
\[{I_{{\mathbf{rms}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}251.2{\mathbf{ \times }}{10^{ - {\mathbf{2}}}} \approx 2.5\,A\]

Question: An inductor 20 mH, a capacitor 100 μ F and a resistor $50\Omega $ re connected in series across a source of emf, V = 10 sin 314 t. The power loss in the circuit is
Solution:
${P_{av}} = {\left( {\frac{{{V_{RMS}}}}{Z}} \right)^2}R$
$$Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {\omega L - \frac{1}{{\omega C}}} \right)}^2}}  = 56\,\Omega $$
$\therefore {P_{av}} = {\left( {\frac{{10}}{{\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)56}}} \right)^2} \times 50 = 0.79W$

Question: A series LCR circuit with inductance 10 H, capacitance 10μF, resistance 50Ω is connected to an ac source of voltage, V =200 sin(100 t)volt. If the resonant frequency of the LCR circuit is v<sub>o</sub> and the frequency of the ac source is v, then :
Solution:
$V = 200\sin \left( {100t} \right)$
$\begin{gathered}
  \omega = 100 \\
   \Rightarrow 2\pi v = 100 \\
   \Rightarrow v = \frac{{50}}{\pi }{H_2} \\
\end{gathered} $
For resonance
$\begin{gathered}
  2\pi {v_0}L = \frac{1}{{2\pi {v_0}c}} \\
  {v_0} = \frac{1}{{2\pi \sqrt {Lc} }} = \frac{1}{{2\pi \sqrt {10 \times 10 \times 10} }} = 6 \\
\end{gathered} $
${v_0} = \frac{{{{10}^3}}}{{20\pi }} = \frac{{50}}{\pi }{H_2}$

Question: A resistance ‘R’ draws power ‘P’ when connected to an AC source. If an inductance is now placed in series with the resistance, such that the impedance of the circuit becomes ‘Z’, the power drawn will be:
Solution:
A resistance R draws power P when connected to an AC source.
The magnitude of voltage of the AC source is V2 = RP
\[\therefore V = \sqrt {PR} \]
An inductor of inductance L and reactance ωL is now placed in series with the resistance. The impedance Z is given by
\[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {\omega ^2}{L^2}} \]

$$\tan \phi  = \frac{{\omega L}}{R};{\tan ^2}\phi  = \frac{{{\omega ^2}{L^2}}}{{{R^2}}}$$

$$1 + {\tan ^2}\phi  = 1 + \frac{{{\omega ^2}{L^2}}}{{{R^2}}} = \frac{{{R^2} + {\omega ^2}{L^2}}}{{{R^2}}} = {\sec ^2}\phi $$
$${\cos ^2}\phi  = \frac{{{R^2}}}{{{R^2} + {\omega ^2}{L^2}}};\cos \phi  = \frac{R}{{{{\left( {{R^2} + {\omega ^2}{L^2}} \right)}^{1/2}}}} = \frac{R}{Z}$$

Power drawn is \[VI'\cos \emptyset = V\left( {\frac{V}{Z}} \right)\left( {\frac{R}{Z}} \right)\]
\[ = \frac{{{V^2}R}}{{{Z^2}}} = \frac{{{V^2}}}{R}\left( {\frac{{{R^2}}}{{{Z^2}}}} \right) = P{\left( {\frac{R}{Z}} \right)^2}\]

Question: An inductor 20 mH, a capacitor 50μF and a resistor 40 Ω are connected in series across a source of emf V = 10 sin 340 t. The power loss in A.C. circuit is
Solution:
\[{X_C} = \frac{1}{{\omega C}} = \frac{1}{{340 \times 50 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 58.8\,{\mkern 1mu} \Omega \]

\[{X_L}{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}\omega L{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}340{\rm{ }} \times {\rm{ }}20{\rm{ }} \times {\rm{ }}{10^{ - 3}} = {\rm{ }}6.8{\rm{ }}\Omega \]

\[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {{X_C} - {X_L}} \right)}^2}}  = \sqrt {{{40}^2} + {{\left( {58.8 - 6.8} \right)}^2}}  = \sqrt {4304} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \Omega \]

\[P = i_{rms}^2R = {\left( {\frac{{{V_{rms}}}}{Z}} \right)^2}R = {\left( {\frac{{\frac{{10}}{{\sqrt 2 }}}}{{\sqrt {4304} }}} \right)^2} \times 40 = \frac{{50 \times 40}}{{4304}} = 0.47{\mkern 1mu} \,W\]

\[{\rm{So}}\,{\rm{best}}\,{\rm{answer}}\,{\rm{(nearest}}\,{\rm{answer)}}\,{\rm{will}}\,{\rm{be}}\,(a)\]

Question: Nitration of aniline in strong acidic medium also gives m-nitroaniline because
Options: In absence of substituents, nitro group always goes to m-position
In electrophilic substitution reactions, amino group is meta directive
Inspite of substituents, nitro group always goes to only m-position
In acidic (strong) medium, aniline is present as anilinium ion
Solution: $ - \mathop N\limits^ \oplus {H_3}$ is m-directing, hence m-nitroaniline is formed as product.

Question:
Statement I: Primary aliphatic amines react with HNO2 to give unstable diazonium salts.
Statement II: Primary aromatic amines react with HNO2 to form diazonium salts which are stable even above 300 K.
Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
Solution: Primary aliphatic amines form highly unstable diazonium salts as they decompose even at low temperature and primary aromatic amines are unstable above 0-5<sup>o</sup>C i.e., 273-278 K

Question: The electrolytic reduction of nitrobenzene in strongly acidic medium produces:
Solution: Electrolytic reduction of nitrobenzene in weakly acidic medium gives aniline but in strongly acidic medium, it gives para-amino phenol obviously through the acid catalyzed rearrangement of initially formed phenyl hydroxyl amine.

Question: A bulb is rated at 150 watt, converting $8 \%$ energy into light. If energy of one photon is $4.42 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$, how many photons are emitted by the bulb per second?
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Energy } & =\text { Power × time } \\
& =150 \mathrm{watt} \times 1 \mathrm{~s} \\
& =150 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$
Energy converted to light $=\frac{150 \times 8}{100}=12 \mathrm{~J}$
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{nh} \nu$
Energy of one photon $=4.42 \times 10^{-19}$
So, $\mathrm{n}=$ number of photons $=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{h} \nu}=\frac{12}{4.42 \times 10^{-19}}$
$=2.715 \times 10^{19}$

Question: The compound that is most difficult to protonate is:
Solution: Due to involvement of lone pair of electrons in resonance in phenol, it will have positive charge (partial), hence incoming proton will not be able to attack easily.
